## PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between the carbon cycle and the nitrogen cycle with respect to (a) their major reservoir and (b) their most significant anthropogenic disruption.** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Carbon Cycle | Nitrogen Cycle |
|---------|--------------|----------------|
| (a) Major reservoir | Sedimentary rocks (fossil fuels) — long-term vault | Atmosphere (as inert N₂ gas, ~78%) |
| (b) Key anthropogenic disruption | Excessive burning of fossil fuels → ↑ atmospheric CO₂ → global warming | Overuse of synthetic fertilizers → nitrate leaching → eutrophication of water bodies |

> **Examiner Insight:** Always use the word *"anthropogenic"* when discussing human impact. Marks are reserved for distinguishing the *type* of pollution caused (greenhouse effect vs. eutrophication).

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Name the microorganisms responsible for (a) nitrogen fixation in legume root nodules and (b) nitrification of ammonia to nitrite.** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

- **(a)** *Rhizobium* (a symbiotic, Gram-negative bacterium that infects the root hairs of leguminous plants like *Trifolium* and *Pisum*, forming characteristic root nodules where biological N₂ fixation occurs via the **nitrogenase enzyme**).
- **(b)** *Nitrosomonas* (a chemoautotrophic bacterium that performs the first step of nitrification: $\text{NH}_4^+ \rightarrow \text{NO}_2^-$, generating energy for CO₂ assimilation).

> **Examiner Insight:** Spelling of the genus name (*Rhizobium* with a single 'i' before 'b' and *italicised*) carries a mark. Always italicize genus names in handwritten answers.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**"With the help of a neat schematic diagram, explain the various steps of the nitrogen cycle in nature. Discuss the role of microorganisms in each step. Also explain how human activities disrupt the natural nitrogen cycle."**

#### (a) Schematic diagram and steps of the nitrogen cycle (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The nitrogen cycle is a complex biogeochemical cycle involving five major microbial-mediated transformations. The schematic flow is:

$$\text{N}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Fixation}} \text{NH}_3/\text{NH}_4^+ \xrightarrow{\text{Nitrification}} \text{NO}_2^- \xrightarrow{\text{Nitrification}} \text{NO}_3^- \xrightarrow{\text{Assimilation}} \text{Organic N} \xrightarrow{\text{Ammonification}} \text{NH}_4^+ \xrightarrow{\text{Denitrification}} \text{N}_2$$

**Step 1 — Nitrogen Fixation:**
- Atmospheric N₂ is reduced to NH₃.
- Biological: *Rhizobium* (legume symbiosis), *Azotobacter* (free-living), *Cyanobacteria* (e.g., *Anabaena* in *Azolla*).
- Equation: $N_2 + 8H^+ + 8e^- + 16ATP \xrightarrow{\text{Nitrogenase}} 2NH_3 + H_2 + 16ADP + 16P_i$
- Industrial: Haber-Bosch process at **400 °C, 200 atm, Fe catalyst** yields NH₃ for fertilizers.

**Step 2 — Nitrification:**
- Two-step microbial oxidation in aerobic soils.
- *Nitrosomonas europaea*: $\text{NH}_4^+ + 1.5 O_2 \rightarrow \text{NO}_2^- + 2H^+ + H_2O$
- *Nitrobacter winogradskyi*: $\text{NO}_2^- + 0.5 O_2 \rightarrow \text{NO}_3^-$

**Step 3 — Assimilation:**
- Plant roots absorb NO₃⁻/NH₄⁺.
- Inside plant cells, NO₃⁻ is reduced to NH₄⁺ by **nitrate reductase** and **nitrite reductase**.
- NH₄⁺ is incorporated into **glutamine** and **glutamate** (the nitrogen donors for all amino acids, nucleotides, and chlorophyll).

**Step 4 — Ammonification (Mineralization):**
- Dead organic matter is decomposed by saprophytic bacteria and fungi.
- Proteins $\rightarrow$ amino acids $\rightarrow$ NH₃/NH₄⁺ (via deamination).
- Key microbes: *Bacillus*, *Clostridium*, *Proteus*.

**Step 5 — Denitrification:**
- Anaerobic bacteria (*Pseudomonas*, *Bacillus*, *Paracoccus*) use NO₃⁻ as a terminal electron acceptor in place of O₂.
- Equation: $C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6NO_3^- \rightarrow 6CO_2 + 3N_2 + 6H_2O$

**Step 6 — Anammox (Modern Discovery):**
- *Planctomycetes* bacteria combine NH₄⁺ with NO₂⁻ to form N₂ in anoxic marine sediments.
- Equation: $\text{NH}_4^+ + \text{NO}_2^- \rightarrow \text{N}_2 + 2H_2O$

#### (b) Human disruptions of the nitrogen cycle (7 Marks)

1. **Haber-Bosch Overload:** Industrial N₂ fixation has doubled the natural rate of nitrogen entering the biosphere. Annual fertilizer production exceeds **~120 million tonnes of N** globally.
2. **Eutrophication:** Excess NO₃⁻ leaches into water bodies, causing algal blooms. Decomposition depletes dissolved O₂, creating **hypoxic dead zones** (e.g., Gulf of Mexico, ~15,000 km²).
3. **Greenhouse Gas Emissions:** Fertilizer use and fossil fuel combustion release **N₂O** (nitrous oxide), which has **~300 times the global warming potential of CO₂** over 100 years.
4. **Acid Rain:** NOx from vehicles and power plants forms **HNO₃** in the atmosphere, lowering rainwater pH below 5.6.
5. **Groundwater Contamination:** NO₃⁻ in drinking water causes **methemoglobinemia** ("blue baby syndrome") in infants.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Many students write only the **steps** and forget the **microorganism names**. Marks are explicitly split as: 3 marks for naming microbes, 4 marks for equations/steps, 4 marks for anthropogenic disruption, 3 marks for the schematic. **Always include the diagram even if not asked.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**"Describe the sulphur cycle with a suitable diagram. Explain the role of microorganisms in sulphur transformations. Discuss the causes and consequences of acid rain."**

#### (a) Sulphur cycle diagram and microbial transformations (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The sulphur cycle is a **hybrid cycle** with both sedimentary and gaseous components. The major reservoirs are the lithosphere (pyrite, gypsum), the hydrosphere (dissolved SO₄²⁻), and the atmosphere (SO₂, H₂S).

**Step 1 — Weathering of Mineral Sulphur:**
- Pyrite (FeS₂) and gypsum (CaSO₄·2H₂O) undergo chemical weathering:
  $2FeS_2 + 7O_2 + 2H_2O \rightarrow 2Fe^{2+} + 4SO_4^{2-} + 4H^+$
- This is the major source of SO₄²⁻ in rivers and oceans.

**Step 2 — Assimilation by Plants:**
- Plant roots absorb SO₄²⁻ and reduce it to S²⁻ for incorporation into cysteine and methionine.

**Step 3 — Decomposition (Ammonification analogue):**
- Microbial mineralization of organic S releases H₂S and S²⁻.

**Step 4 — Microbial Sulphur Oxidation:**
- *Thiobacillus thiooxidans*, *Beggiatoa*, *Sulfolobus* oxidize H₂S to elemental S or SO₄²⁻.
- $2H_2S + O_2 \rightarrow 2S + 2H_2O$ (Beggiatoa deposits intracellular S granules).
- $2S + 3O_2 + 2H_2O \rightarrow 2SO_4^{2-} + 4H^+$ (Thiobacillus).

**Step 5 — Microbial Sulphate Reduction:**
- *Desulfovibrio desulfuricans* and *Desulfobacter* are obligate anaerobes that reduce SO₄²⁻ to H₂S using organic matter as electron donor:
  $SO_4^{2-} + 2CH_2O \rightarrow H_2S + 2HCO_3^-$

**Step 6 — Atmospheric Phase:**
- SO₂ is emitted by volcanic eruptions, fossil fuel combustion, and biomass burning.
- $S + O_2 \rightarrow SO_2$
- $2SO_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2SO_3$
- $SO_3 + H_2O \rightarrow H_2SO_4$ (sulphuric acid — the primary acid rain component)

#### (b) Acid Rain — causes and consequences (7 Marks)

**Causes:**
1. **Anthropogenic SO₂ emissions** from coal-fired thermal power plants (India has >200 GW coal capacity).
2. **NOx emissions** from vehicles contribute secondary HNO₃.
3. **Volcanic SO₂** (natural background) and **Dimethyl Sulphide (DMS)** from marine phytoplankton.

**Consequences:**
1. **Soil Acidification:** Leaching of Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, K⁺ nutrients; mobilization of toxic Al³⁺.
2. **Aquatic Ecosystem Damage:** Lowered pH in lakes kills fish eggs and amphibians; e.g., the "acid rain crisis" of the 1980s killed fish in Scandinavian and North American lakes.
3. **Forest Decline:** Damages needle/leaves; weakens trees against pests (e.g., German *Waldsterben*).
4. **Building Corrosion:** Limestone (CaCO₃) and marble monuments dissolve:
   $CaCO_3 + H_2SO_4 \rightarrow CaSO_4 + H_2O + CO_2$
5. **Human Health:** Fine sulphate aerosols cause respiratory diseases (asthma, bronchitis).
6. **Visibility Reduction:** Sulphate aerosols scatter light, causing regional haze.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks Loss Alert:**
> - ✗ Drawing the diagram *without* a **legend/colour coding** of reservoirs (atmosphere, lithosphere, biosphere, hydrosphere) — loses 1 mark.
> - ✗ Writing "sulphur cycle" without **italicizing genus names** like *Thiobacillus* — loses 0.5–1 mark per name.
> - ✗ Omitting the **anthropogenic impact** section even when the question is descriptive — KTU 2024 scheme now mandates **environmental linkage**.

---

## KTU EXAMINATION QUICK-REFERENCE — IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print-and-Carry)**

### 🔵 Carbon Cycle
- **Reservoirs:** Atmosphere (~870 Gt), Ocean (~38,000 Gt), Fossil fuels (~10⁸ Gt).
- **Key Process:** Photosynthesis ($6CO_2 + 6H_2O \rightarrow C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2$).
- **Methanogenesis:** Done by *Methanobacterium* in anaerobic conditions.
- **Residence time of CO₂ in atmosphere:** ~5–6 years.
- **Disruption:** Fossil fuel burning → Global warming (CO₂ is a greenhouse gas).
- **Current atmospheric CO₂:** **~420 ppm** (rising ~2.4 ppm/year).

### 🟢 Nitrogen Cycle
- **Atmosphere is 78% N₂** but biologically inert.
- **Nitrogenase enzyme** catalyzes fixation (highly O₂-sensitive).
- **Symbiotic fixer:** *Rhizobium*; **Free-living fixers:** *Azotobacter*, *Clostridium pasteurianum*, *Cyanobacteria* (*Anabaena*).
- **Nitrification:** *Nitrosomonas* (NH₄⁺ → NO₂⁻), *Nitrobacter* (NO₂⁻ → NO₃⁻).
- **Denitrification:** *Pseudomonas*, *Bacillus*, *Thiobacillus denitrificans*.
- **Haber-Bosch:** N₂ + 3H₂ → 2NH₃ (400 °C, 200 atm, Fe catalyst).
- **Disruption:** Fertilizer runoff → **eutrophication** and **N₂O emission** (GHG).
- **Anammox** = Anaerobic Ammonium Oxidation, by *Planctomycetes*.

### 🟡 Sulphur Cycle
- **Reservoirs:** Lithosphere (pyrite, gypsum — major); Atmosphere (SO₂, H₂S).
- **Sulphide minerals:** Pyrite (FeS₂), Galena (PbS), Sphalerite (ZnS).
- **Sulphur oxidizers:** *Thiobacillus thiooxidans*, *Beggiatoa alba*.
- **Sulphate reducers:** *Desulfovibrio desulfuricans* (anaerobic).
- **Acid Rain:** $SO_2 + 0.5O_2 \rightarrow SO_3$; $SO_3 + H_2O \rightarrow H_2SO_4$.
- **Acid rain pH:** < 5.6 (normal rainwater pH = 5.6 due to dissolved CO₂).
- **Damage:** Marble corrosion, forest decline, fish kills, soil nutrient leaching.
- **Control:** FGD scrubbers in power plants, low-sulphur fuels, catalytic converters.

### 🔴 Universal Mnemonics
- **C–N–S** cycles in this order for exam writing.
- **"Plants Pull, Microbes Make, Mankind MarS"** — easy recall.
- **Fix → Nitrify → Assimilate → Ammonify → Denitrify** — the canonical nitrogen sequence.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Strategy (KTU 2024):**
> 1. Always begin long-answer questions with a **labelled diagram** (3–4 marks reserved).
> 2. Quote **at least one balanced chemical equation** per cycle.
> 3. **Name at least 2 microorganisms** with their specific roles.
> 4. End with a **concluding paragraph on anthropogenic impact** — a mandatory expectation under NEP 2020 / OBE (Outcome-Based Education) frameworks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Microbiological Analysis of Water: Sanitary Tests for Coliforms (Presumptive, Confirmed, Completed Tests, and IMViC Tests)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBBT303 - ENGINEERING) > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Microbiological Analysis of Water: Sanitary Tests for Coliforms (Presumptive, Confirmed, Completed Tests, and IMViC Tests) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What are Coliforms? **Coliforms** are a group of Gram-negative, non-spore forming, rod-shaped bacteria that ferment lactose with the production of acid and gas within **48 hours at 35 °C**. They belong to the family *Enterobacteriaceae* and are universally present in the intestinal tract of warm-blooded animals, hence in their faeces. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Coliforms are **indicator organisms** — they are used to detect faecal pollution in water because they are: > - Universally present in large numbers in faeces of warm-blooded animals > - Easily detectable by simple laboratory tests > - Survive longer in water than pathogens > - Do not multiply appreciably in natural water **Two major categories of coliforms used in water analysis:** | Category | Description | Examples | |----------|-------------|----------| | **Total Coliforms** | All coliforms (faecal + non-faecal origin) | *Escherichia, Citrobacter, Enterobacter, Klebsiella* | | **Faecal Coliforms (FC)** | Coliforms that grow at 44.5 °C — indicate recent faecal pollution | *E. coli* (typical) | ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a swimming pool. You cannot test it for *every* dangerous pathogen (typhoid, cholera, hepatitis A virus) — there are hundreds of them, and they are hard to detect. Instead, you test for **"tracer bullets"** — coliforms. Think of coliforms as the **"canary in the coal mine"** 🐤. Just as coal miners carried canaries to detect toxic gases (canaries die first), coliforms serve as biological "canaries" that warn us of faecal contamination. If coliforms are present, it strongly suggests that faecal material — and potentially disease-causing pathogens — have also entered the water. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Permissible Limit (BIS / WHO):** > - Drinking water: **0 MPN/100 mL** (must be free from coliforms) > - Total coliforms in piped water: **0/100 mL** > - Faecal coliforms: **0/100 mL** > - MPN is the unit of measurement (Most Probable Number per 100 mL) --- ### 1.3 Standard Methods of Coliform Detection — Overview The classical **Multiple Tube Fermentation (MTF) technique** uses a three-stage test sequence: 1. **Presumptive Test** → detects *presumptive* presence of coliforms 2. **Confirmed Test** → confirms their presence 3. **Completed Test** → confirms identity of *E. coli* These are followed by **IMViC biochemical tests** to differentiate between members of the coliform group (especially *E. coli* from *Enterobacter aerogenes*). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 THE PRESUMPTIVE TEST **Principle:** The water sample is inoculated into lactose broth (with Durham tube) and incubated at 35 °C for 24–48 hours. If coliforms are present, they ferment lactose producing **acid (lowering pH) and gas (CO₂ + H₂)** collected in the inverted Durham tube. > [!NOTE] > **Why acid AND gas?** > - **Acid** is detected by colour change of broth due to fermentation > - **Gas** is detected as a bubble trapped in the inverted Durham tube > - Both being positive = strong presumptive evidence of coliforms **Procedure:** 1. Inoculate **15 tubes** of lactose broth in **3 sets of 5 tubes each** with: - Set 1: **10 mL** of double-strength broth + **1 mL** sample - Set 2: **10 mL** of single-strength broth + **1 mL** of 1:10 dilution - Set 3: **10 mL** of single-strength broth + **1 mL** of 1:100 dilution 2. Incubate at **35 ± 0.5 °C for 24–48 hours**. 3. Observe: **acid (yellow colour)** + **gas (10% or more in Durham tube)** = positive. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Composition of Lactose Broth:** Peptone (5 g/L), Beef extract (3 g/L), Lactose (5 g/L), Bromocresol purple indicator. > - 15 tubes, 3 dilutions, 5 tubes per dilution → **MPN Index** (per 100 mL) **MPN (Most Probable Number)** is a statistical estimate of bacterial density based on probability theory (based on Poisson distribution). ### 2.2 THE CONFIRMED TEST **Purpose:** To confirm that the gas-producing organisms in the presumptive test are **actually coliforms** and not anaerobes / other gas-producers. **Procedure:** - From each **positive presumptive tube**, transfer one loopful to: - **Brilliant Green Lactose Bile (BGLB) broth** — incubated at 35 °C for 48 h, OR - **Eosin Methylene Blue (EMB) agar** — incubated at 35 °C for 24 h - **BGLB broth** selectively inhibits Gram-positive and some Gram-negative bacteria, allowing coliforms to grow. - **EMB agar** produces characteristic colonies: - *E. coli* → **metallic green sheen** (24 h) - *Enterobacter* / *Klebsiella* → **pink/mucoid** colonies - Non-coliforms → colourless > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students confuse the temperatures. Remember: > - 35 °C = Total coliforms > - 44.5 °C = Faecal coliforms (thermotolerant) ### 2.3 THE COMPLETED TEST **Purpose:** To confirm that the organism is *Escherichia coli* (the principal faecal coliform). **Procedure:** 1. From each positive BGLB/EMB tube/plate, streak onto **nutrient agar slant** AND inoculate into **lactose broth with Durham tube**. 2. Incubate at 35 °C for 24 h. 3. Check: - **Lactose broth**: acid + gas (+) - **Nutrient agar**: examine colony morphology 4. Perform **Gram staining** → Gram-negative, non-spore forming rods 5. Examine under microscope → confirms the coliform morphology > [!NOTE] > A **positive completed test** = isolated organism is *E. coli* (faecal coliform). ### 2.4 THE IMViC TESTS The **IMViC** is a battery of **four biochemical tests** used to **differentiate** members of the *Enterobacteriaceae* family, especially to distinguish: - **E. coli** (faecal) vs. **Enterobacter aerogenes** (non-faecal / soil) | Letter | Test | Detects | Reagent | |--------|------|---------|---------| | **I** | **I**ndole | Tryptophan → Indole | Kovac's reagent | | **M** | **M**ethyl Red | Mixed acid fermentation | Methyl Red indicator | | **V** | **V**oges-Proskauer | Acetoin production | α-naphthol + KOH (40%) | | **C** | **C**itrate | Citrate utilization (as sole C-source) | Simmon's Citrate agar + Bromothymol blue | > [!IMPORTANT] > The "**i**" in IMViC is for ease of pronunciation, and **V & P** are the **Voges-Proskauer** test (often denoted as **Vi**). ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet $$ \boxed{\text{MPN Index} = \text{Number of organisms per 100 mL}} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{Permissible MPN} = 0 \text{ per } 100 \text{ mL of drinking water}} $$ **IMViC Pattern Comparison (Most Important for KTU):** | Organism | Indole | MR | VP | Citrate | Origin | |----------|:------:|:--:|:--:|:-------:|--------| | *Escherichia coli* | **+** | **+** | **−** | **−** | Faecal | | *Enterobacter aerogenes* | **−** | **−** | **+** | **+** | Non-faecal (soil) | | *Klebsiella pneumoniae* | **−** | **−** | **+** | **+** | Soil | | *Citrobacter freundii* | **−** | **+** | **−** | **+** | Variable | | *Klebsiella oxytoca* | **+** | **−** | **+** | **+** | Variable | > [!NOTE] > **Classic KTU-asked mnemonic:** > "**+ + − −**" = *E. coli* (the most important pattern to remember!) **MPN Table (Most Common 3-Tube Set used in KTU exams):** | Combination of Positives (3 sets of 5 tubes) | MPN Index / 100 mL | |:--------------------------------------------:|:------------------:| | 5-5-5 | ≥ 1600 | | 5-5-4 | 1300 | | 5-5-3 | 950 | | 5-5-2 | 700 | | 5-5-1 | 400 | | 5-5-0 | 240 | | 5-4-5 | 1100 | | 5-4-4 | 850 | | 5-4-3 | 600 | | 5-4-2 | 440 | | 5-4-1 | 250 | | 5-4-0 | 170 | | 5-3-3 | 390 | | 5-3-2 | 290 | | 5-3-1 | 170 | | 5-3-0 | 130 | | 5-2-2 | 220 | | 5-2-1 | 130 | | 5-2-0 | 90 | | 5-1-1 | 70 | | 5-1-0 | 40 | | 5-0-0 | 20 | | 0-0-0 | <2 | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Mathematical Basis of MPN The MPN method is based on the **Poisson distribution** of bacterial occurrences. The probability of a tube showing no growth is given by: $$ P(\text{no growth}) = e^{-\lambda} $$ where $\lambda$ is the mean number of organisms per tube volume. The probability of a tube showing growth (i.e., positive) is: $$ P(\text{growth}) = 1 - e^{-\lambda} $$ **Derivation of the Most Probable Number:** If a tube of volume $V$ contains $n$ bacteria on average, then for $k$ positive tubes out of $n_t$ tubes, the probability is given by the **binomial-like expansion**: $$ P_{\text{positive}} = 1 - e^{-n V} $$ For three dilutions, let: - $n_1, n_2, n_3$ = number of positive tubes in sets 1, 2, 3 (each set has 5 tubes) - $V_1, V_2, V_3$ = volume in each tube of the respective set The MPN is computed as: $$ \boxed{\text{MPN} = \frac{P_1 \cdot P_2 \cdot P_3 \cdot 100}{\sqrt{V_1 \cdot V_2 \cdot V_3}}} $$ where $P_i$ is the count of positive tubes in set $i$. > [!NOTE] > In KTU exam questions, you will typically be **given the MPN value from the standard MPN table** rather than calculating it from scratch. So memorise the MPN table values. ### 3.2 Worked Example — MPN Determination **Problem:** A water sample was analysed using the 5-tube MPN method. Results: - 10 mL sample: 5 tubes positive - 1 mL sample: 3 tubes positive - 0.1 mL sample: 2 tubes positive Find the MPN index per 100 mL. **Solution:** **Step 1:** Identify the positive combination → **5-3-2** **Step 2:** Refer to the standard MPN table. $$ \text{MPN Index} = 290 \text{ per } 100 \text{ mL} $$ **Step 3:** Interpret the result. > [!WARNING] > **Exceeds WHO/BIS limit of 0/100 mL → Water is NOT potable. Significant faecal contamination.** ### 3.3 Worked Example — IMViC Pattern Identification **Problem:** A bacterial isolate from water gives the following results: - Indole test: Red ring on Kovac's reagent → **Positive** - Methyl Red test: Red colour persists → **Positive** - Voges-Proskauer test: No pink colour → **Negative** - Citrate test: No growth, no colour change → **Negative** Identify the organism and its significance. **Solution:** **Step 1:** Note the IMViC pattern → **+ + − −** **Step 2:** Match with the standard IMViC table. $$ \text{Indole} = +,\ \text{MR} = +,\ \text{VP} = -,\ \text{Citrate} = - $$ **Step 3:** The pattern **+ + − −** corresponds to ***Escherichia coli***. **Step 4:** Significance: *E. coli* is the **primary indicator of recent faecal contamination**. The water is **unsafe for drinking** without treatment. ### 3.4 Step-by-Step IMViC Test Chemistry #### (a) Indole Test **Reaction:** Tryptophan (from peptone) is hydrolysed by the enzyme **tryptophanase** to indole, pyruvic acid, and ammonia. $$ \text{Tryptophan} \xrightarrow{\text{tryptophanase}} \text{Indole} + \text{Pyruvic acid} + \text{NH}_3 $$ **Detection:** Kovac's reagent (p-dimethylaminobenzaldehyde in amyl alcohol + HCl) reacts with indole to form a **cherry-red ring** (rosindole dye). $$ \text{Indole} + \text{Kovac's reagent} \longrightarrow \text{Red Ring (rosindole)} $$ #### (b) Methyl Red (MR) Test **Reaction:** Detects **mixed acid fermentation** (production of large amounts of stable acids from glucose). $$ \text{Glucose} \xrightarrow{\text{fermentation}} \text{Acetic, Lactic, Formic, Succinic acids} $$ **Detection:** Methyl Red indicator at pH 4.4 → **red colour** (acidic) or yellow (neutral). #### (c) Voges-Proskauer (VP) Test **Reaction:** Detects **acetoin (acetylmethylcarbinol)** produced from glucose fermentation via the **2,3-butanediol pathway**. $$ 2 \text{ Pyruvate} \longrightarrow \text{Acetoin} + 2 \text{CO}_2 $$ **Detection:** α-naphthol + KOH oxidize acetoin to diacetyl, which gives a **pink/red colour**. #### (d) Citrate Utilization Test **Reaction:** Organisms that produce the enzyme **citrate permease** can use citrate as the sole carbon source. $$ \text{Citrate} \xrightarrow{\text{citrate permease}} \text{Oxaloacetate} \rightarrow \text{Pyruvate} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$ **Detection:** Simmon's citrate agar + bromothymol blue indicator. Growth + blue colour = **positive**. ### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Feature | E. coli | Enterobacter aerogenes | |---------|---------|------------------------| | Origin | Faeces (warm-blooded animals) | Soil, vegetation | | IMViC | + + − − | − − + + | | Lactose fermentation | + (acid + gas) | + (acid + gas) | | Growth at 44.5 °C | + | − | | Public health significance | **High** — recent faecal pollution | Low — environmental | | MPN in drinking water | **Must be 0/100 mL** | Not regulated | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Workflow Diagram — Complete Sanitary Test for Coliforms ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Water Sample Collection
Sterile Bottle, 100-200 mL] --> B[STEP 1: Presumptive Test
Lactose Broth + Durham Tube
35 deg C / 24-48 h] B --> C{Gas Production
Observed?} C -->|No| D[Negative - Water is Safe
from Coliforms] C -->|Yes| E[STEP 2: Confirmed Test
Transfer to BGLB Broth or EMB Agar
35 deg C / 24-48 h] E --> F{BGLB: Gas +
EMB: Metallic Sheen?} F -->|No| D F -->|Yes| G[STEP 3: Completed Test
Nutrient Agar Slant + Lactose Broth
35 deg C / 24 h] G --> H{Gram Negative Rods
Acid + Gas in LB?} H -->|No| D H -->|Yes| I[STEP 4: IMViC Tests
Differentiate E. coli vs. Enterobacter] I --> J{IMViC Pattern?} J -->|++--| K[E. coli Confirmed
RECENT FAECAL CONTAMINATION
Water UNSAFE] J -->|----| L[Enterobacter aerogenes
NON-FAECAL ORIGIN
Soil/Vegetation] J -->|Other Patterns| M[Other Coliforms
Requires Further Analysis] style A fill:#e1f5ff style B fill:#fff3cd style E fill:#fff3cd style G fill:#fff3cd style I fill:#fff3cd style K fill:#f8d7da style L fill:#d4edda style D fill:#d4edda ``` ### 4.2 IMViC Test Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Isolate from Confirmed Test] --> B[Inoculate Four Test Media] B --> C1[Indole Test
Kovac's Reagent] B --> C2[Methyl Red Test
MR Indicator] B --> C3[VP Test
alpha-Naphthol + KOH] B --> C4[Citrate Test
Simmon's Citrate Agar] C1 --> D1{Indole} C2 --> D2{MR} C3 --> D3{VP} C4 --> D4{Citrate} D1 -->|Positive| E1[Indole Positive] D1 -->|Negative| E2[Indole Negative] D2 -->|Positive| F1[MR Positive] D2 -->|Negative| F2[MR Negative] D3 -->|Positive| G1[VP Positive] D3 -->|Negative| G2[VP Negative] D4 -->|Positive| H1[Citrate Positive] D4 -->|Negative| H2[Citrate Negative] E1 --> X1[Combine Results] F1 --> X1 G2 --> X1 H2 --> X1 X1 --> R1[++-- = E. coli] E2 --> X2[Combine Results] F2 --> X2 G1 --> X2 H1 --> X2 X2 --> R2[--++ = Enterobacter aerogenes] style R1 fill:#ffcccc style R2 fill:#ccffcc ``` ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Water Quality Decision System ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Sampling Point] --> A2[Sample Container
Sterile Glass] A2 --> A3[Lab Reception
4 deg C Storage] A3 --> A4[Test Battery Selection] A4 --> B1[Lactose Broth Presumptive] A4 --> B2[EMB Agar Plate] A4 --> B3[Biochemical IMViC Set] A1 --> C1[Physical Tests
pH, Turbidity, TDS] A1 --> C2[Chemical Tests
BOD, COD, Chlorine] B1 --> D1[Gas Production Analysis] B2 --> D2[Colony Morphology] B3 --> D3[IMViC Pattern Engine] D1 --> E1[MPN Calculation Module] D2 --> E2[Differentiation Engine] D3 --> E2 E1 --> F1[MPN per 100 mL] E2 --> F2[Organism Identification] F1 --> G1[Decision Matrix] F2 --> G1 C1 --> G1 C2 --> G1 G1 --> H1{Safe for
Consumption?} H1 -->|Yes| I1[Approved for Supply] H1 -->|No| I2[Reject / Treat Further] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — 3-Mark Questions --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** Define coliforms. Why are they used as indicator organisms in water analysis? **Model Answer:** **Coliforms** are defined as Gram-negative, non-spore forming, rod-shaped bacteria belonging to the family *Enterobacteriaceae* that ferment lactose with the production of acid and gas within 48 hours at 35 °C. **They are used as indicator organisms because:** 1. They are universally present in the intestinal tract and faeces of warm-blooded animals. 2. They are present in **large numbers** in faeces (10⁶–10⁹ per gram), making detection easy. 3. They are **easy to detect** by simple, inexpensive tests. 4. They survive in water **longer than pathogenic bacteria** (typhoid, cholera). 5. They do **not multiply appreciably** in natural waters. 6. Their presence indicates that **pathogens may also be present** in the same water. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Differentiate between total coliforms and faecal coliforms. **Model Answer:** | Feature | Total Coliforms | Faecal Coliforms | |---------|-----------------|-------------------| | **Definition** | All coliforms fermenting lactose at 35 °C | Coliforms fermenting lactose at 44.5 °C | | **Origin** | Faeces + soil + vegetation | Specifically faeces of warm-blooded animals | | **Examples** | *E. coli, Enterobacter, Klebsiella, Citrobacter* | *E. coli* (predominant) | | **Temperature** | 35 ± 0.5 °C | 44.5 ± 0.2 °C | | **Significance** | General pollution indicator | Recent faecal pollution indicator | | **Detection medium** | Lactose broth | EC broth / BGLB at 44.5 °C | --- ### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]** **(a)** Describe the **Presumptive Test** for coliforms with procedure and interpretation. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A water sample gave MPN positive combination of **5-3-1** in 5-tube MPN test. Identify the MPN value, interpret the result, and discuss its significance for water potability. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Presumptive Test:** The presumptive test is the first step in the **Multiple Tube Fermentation (MTF) technique** for detecting coliforms in water. **Principle:** Coliforms ferment lactose to produce acid (detected by pH indicator) and gas (CO₂ + H₂) collected in inverted Durham tubes. **Apparatus:** Lactose broth (single and double strength), Durham tubes, sterile pipettes, incubator (35 °C). **Procedure:** 1. Prepare **three sets of 5 tubes** each containing 10 mL of lactose broth: - **Set 1:** Double-strength broth + **10 mL** water sample - **Set 2:** Single-strength broth + **1 mL** water sample - **Set 3:** Single-strength broth + **0.1 mL** water sample 2. Each tube contains an **inverted Durham tube** for gas collection. 3. Incubate at **35 ± 0.5 °C for 24–48 hours**. 4. Observe for **acid production** (yellow colour) and **gas production** (bubble in Durham tube). **Interpretation:** - **Negative result:** No gas in any tube → No coliforms (water is likely safe). - **Positive result:** Gas in Durham tube + acid → Coliforms **presumptively present** → MPN calculated from number of positive tubes. - **Doubtful result:** Only acid, no gas → Inconclusive, repeat. **Significance:** A positive presumptive test indicates **possible** coliform contamination and triggers further confirmed and completed tests. --- **(b) MPN Interpretation (5-3-1 combination):** **Step 1:** Look up the standard MPN table for the combination **5-3-1**. From the MPN reference table: $$ \text{MPN Index} = 170 \text{ per } 100 \text{ mL} $$ **Step 2: Interpretation:** The presence of **170 coliforms per 100 mL** of water indicates **heavy faecal contamination**. **Step 3: Significance for Potability:** > [!WARNING] > - WHO/BIS permissible limit for drinking water: **0 MPN/100 mL** > - This sample has **170 MPN/100 mL** → **Water is UNFIT for drinking.** > - Action required: > - Reject this water source OR > - Mandatory disinfection (chlorination) before supply OR > - Further confirmatory testing (Confirmed + Completed tests) to confirm the coliform identity **Step 4: Public Health Implication:** A high MPN value of 170/100 mL indicates that disease-causing pathogens (typhoid, cholera, dysentery) are also likely present, posing **severe health risk** to consumers. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022] — Alternative Choice** **(a)** Explain the **IMViC tests** with biochemical reactions and their importance in differentiating coliforms. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Differentiate between ***E. coli*** and ***Enterobacter aerogenes*** using the IMViC test pattern. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) IMViC Tests:** IMViC is a battery of four biochemical tests used to differentiate members of the family *Enterobacteriaceae*, especially *E. coli* from *Enterobacter aerogenes*. **I — Indole Test:** **Biochemical Reaction:** Tryptophan (from peptone) is cleaved by **tryptophanase** enzyme into indole, pyruvic acid, and ammonia. $$ \text{Tryptophan} \xrightarrow{\text{tryptophanase}} \text{Indole} + \text{Pyruvate} + \text{NH}_3 $$ **Reagent:** Kovac's reagent (p-DMAB in amyl alcohol + HCl). A **cherry-red ring** at the top indicates **positive result**. --- **M — Methyl Red (MR) Test:** **Biochemical Reaction:** Detects **mixed acid fermentation** of glucose producing stable acids (acetic, formic, lactic). $$ \text{Glucose} \xrightarrow{\text{mixed acid fermentation}} \text{Acetic + Formic + Lactic acids} $$ **Indicator:** Methyl Red. **Red colour** at pH 4.4 = **positive**; yellow = negative. --- **V — Voges-Proskauer (VP) Test:** **Biochemical Reaction:** Detects **acetoin (acetylmethylcarbinol)** produced via the **2,3-butanediol pathway** from glucose. $$ 2 \text{ Pyruvate} \longrightarrow \text{Acetoin} + 2\text{CO}_2 $$ **Reagent:** α-naphthol + 40% KOH. **Pink-red colour** in 5–30 min = **positive**. --- **C — Citrate Utilization Test:** **Biochemical Reaction:** Organisms possessing **citrate permease** use citrate as sole carbon source. $$ \text{Citrate} \xrightarrow{\text{citrate permease}} \text{Oxaloacetate} \rightarrow \text{Pyruvate} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$ **Medium:** Simmon's Citrate Agar with bromothymol blue indicator. **Growth + blue colour** = **positive**. --- **Importance of IMViC Tests:** 1. Differentiates faecal (*E. coli*) from non-faecal (*Enterobacter*) coliforms. 2. Confirms identity of pathogens in water. 3. Helps trace the source of water pollution (faecal vs. environmental). 4. Essential for epidemiological investigations of waterborne diseases. --- **(b) IMViC Differentiation of *E. coli* vs. *Enterobacter aerogenes*:** | Test | *E. coli* | *E. aerogenes* | Result Interpretation | |------|:---------:|:--------------:|----------------------| | **I**ndole | **+** | **−** | *E. coli* hydrolyses tryptophan | | **M**R | **+** | **−** | *E. coli* performs mixed acid fermentation | | **V**P | **−** | **+** | *E. aerogenes* performs 2,3-butanediol fermentation | | **C**itrate | **−** | **+** | *E. aerogenes* uses citrate as carbon source | | **Pattern** | **+ + − −** | **− − + +** | — | **Key Differences:** 1. **Origin:** *E. coli* originates from **faeces of warm-blooded animals** (recent contamination), while *E. aerogenes* originates from **soil and vegetation** (old/environmental pollution). 2. **Health Significance:** *E. coli* (++−−) is a **definitive indicator of faecal pollution** and indicates **high health risk**. *E. aerogenes* (−−++) indicates **non-faecal/environmental pollution** with **lower health risk**. 3. **Biochemical Pathway:** *E. coli* uses **mixed acid pathway**; *E. aerogenes* uses **butanediol pathway**. 4. **Optimal Temperature:** *E. coli* grows at **44.5 °C** (faecal coliform); *E. aerogenes* does not. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Do NOT confuse the order** of IMViC reactions — write them as **I, M, V, C** in that order. > - **Always state both positives and negatives** for each organism in comparison tables. > - The **+ + − −** pattern for *E. coli* is the **most-asked** IMViC pattern. Memorize it perfectly. > - Do **NOT** confuse **Citrate test** (IMViC) with **Catalase test** — they are different. > - In MPN problems, always read the MPN value from the **standard 5-tube MPN table** — do not guess. > - Mention **temperature (35 °C for total, 44.5 °C for faecal)** explicitly in confirmed tests. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀 > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist:** ✅ **Coliforms** = Gram (−) rods, ferment lactose at 35 °C / 48 h producing acid + gas. ✅ **Indicator organisms** because they indicate **faecal pollution** (pathogen presence). ✅ **Three stages of MTF:** Presumptive → Confirmed → Completed. ✅ **Presumptive Test:** Lactose broth + Durham tube, 35 °C / 24–48 h, look for **acid + gas**. ✅ **Confirmed Test:** Transfer to BGLB broth or EMB agar; look for **metallic green sheen** (*E. coli*). ✅ **Completed Test:** Re-streak on nutrient agar + lactose broth + Gram staining → confirms *E. coli*. ✅ **IMViC = Indole + Methyl Red + Voges-Proskauer + Citrate.** ✅ ***E. coli* pattern: + + − −** (faecal, dangerous) | ***E. aerogenes*: − − + +** (soil, less dangerous). ✅ **Indole test:** Tryptophan → Indole; **Kovac's reagent → red ring**. ✅ **MR test:** Mixed acid fermentation; **Red colour = positive**. ✅ **VP test:** 2,3-butanediol pathway; **Acetoin detection, pink = positive**. ✅ **Citrate test:** Simmon's agar; **Blue + growth = positive**. ✅ **MPN (Most Probable Number)** is determined using **5-tube or 3-tube MPN tables** per 100 mL. ✅ **Permissible MPN** in drinking water = **0 / 100 mL** (BIS / WHO). ✅ **Faecal coliforms** grow at **44.5 °C** (thermotolerant); total coliforms at **35 °C**. ✅ **Mnemonic for *E. coli* IMViC:** "**+ + − −** = E. coli" → *E* is the 5th letter, write it like *"double-positive, double-negative"*!

topics.title: Pathogenesis of Water Borne Diseases: Cholera and Typhoid
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT303 - ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pathogenesis of Water Borne Diseases: Cholera and Typhoid

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!NOTE]
> **Pathogenesis** is the biological mechanism by which a disease-causing microorganism (pathogen) infects a host, evades the immune defenses, multiplies, and produces the clinical symptoms of the disease. In the context of **water-borne diseases**, pathogenesis specifically traces the journey of a pathogen from a contaminated water source to its colonization of the human gastrointestinal tract and the resulting systemic damage.

**Water-borne diseases** are illnesses caused by the ingestion of water contaminated with pathogenic microorganisms (bacteria, viruses, protozoa, or helminths) or their toxic byproducts. The two classical KTU 2024 Scheme focus diseases are:

| Disease | Causative Agent | Type of Pathogen |
|---|---|---|
| **Cholera** | *Vibrio cholerae* (serogroups O1 & O139) | Gram-negative, comma-shaped bacterium |
| **Typhoid** | *Salmonella enterica* serovar *Typhi* (S. Typhi) | Gram-negative, rod-shaped bacterium |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of your intestine as a fortified city (the body), and the gut epithelium as the city wall.**

**Cholera Analogy — The "Silent Spy that Disables the City Plumbing":**
Imagine a spy (cholera toxin) that does not destroy the city wall, nor harm the guards. Instead, it quietly breaks into the central **water pumping station** of the city (intestinal crypt cells) and reprograms the pumps to throw away water at an uncontrollable rate. The city floods, and people collapse from dehydration — even though the wall is intact. The pathogen itself does not invade; it just secretes a toxin.

**Typhoid Analogy — The "Armed Invader that Crosses the Wall":**
Imagine a heavily armed army (*S. Typhi*) that actually **breaches the city wall** (invades the gut mucosa), kills the guards (M cells, macrophages), and then sets up camp inside the **central government buildings** (liver, spleen, bone marrow). From there, it sends out re-inforcements into the bloodstream, causing systemic fever and organ damage. Unlike the spy in cholera, the typhoid army physically invades the body.

## 1.3 Key Epidemiological Metrics (KTU Board Standard Values)

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 scheme examiners expect the following standard metrics (use exact numbers in answers for full marks):
> - **Infective Dose (ID₅₀)** of *V. cholerae* = **10⁶ – 10⁸ organisms** (high dose, but hyperinfective after shedding).
> - **Infective Dose (ID₅₀)** of *S. Typhi* = **10⁵ – 10⁶ organisms**.
> - **Incubation Period** for Cholera = **2 hours to 5 days** (typically 1–2 days).
> - **Incubation Period** for Typhoid = **6 days to 30 days** (typically 10–14 days).
> - **Basic Reproduction Number (R₀)** for Cholera in an unvaccinated population = **2 – 6** (can exceed 10 in severe outbreaks like Haiti 2010).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Infectious Dose (ID₅₀) is defined as the number of organisms required to infect 50% of a test population. It is a direct measure of pathogen virulence and host resistance.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stage-Wise Pathogenesis of Cholera (*Vibrio cholerae*)

The pathogenesis of cholera follows a **five-stage logical cascade** (must be memorized in order for KTU 14-mark answers):

### Stage 1: Ingestion of Contaminated Water
- Patient ingests water (or food washed in it) containing viable *V. cholerae* in its **acid-sensitive, non-virulent form** (though it survives gastric acid at high doses).
- **Vibrios are highly motile** due to a single polar flagellum, allowing them to swim through the mucus layer.

### Stage 2: Colonization of the Small Intestine
- The bacteria sense the intestinal environment via the **ToxR regulon** and produce critical surface proteins:
  - **Toxin-Coregulated Pilus (TCP)** — bundle-forming pilus essential for microcolony formation.
  - **Cholera Toxin (CT)** — the primary virulence factor.
- Bacteria attach to the **brush border of small intestinal epithelial cells** (especially jejunum and ileum) without invading them.

### Stage 3: Toxin Secretion and Receptor Binding
- Cholera toxin is an **AB₅ exotoxin** (one A subunit + five B subunits).
- The **B subunits bind irreversibly to GM1 ganglioside receptors** on enterocytes.
- The **A subunit is endocytosed** into the cell.

### Stage 4: Molecular Hijack (ADP-Ribosylation Cascade)
- The A subunit catalyzes the transfer of **ADP-ribose from NAD⁺ to the Gsα subunit** of the heterotrimeric G-protein.
- This locks Gsα in its **GTP-bound (active) state**.
- Permanently active Gsα → **continuously activates Adenylate Cyclase** → **massive cAMP accumulation**.

### Stage 5: Water and Electrolyte Efflux
- Elevated cAMP activates the **CFTR (Cystic Fibrosis Transmembrane Conductance Regulator)** channel.
- **Cl⁻ ions flood into the intestinal lumen**.
- Na⁺ and H₂O follow passively → **massive isotonic fluid secretion**.
- Result: **Rice-water stools** (up to 1 liter/hour) leading to severe dehydration, hypovolemic shock, and metabolic acidosis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Point for KTU 2024:** The bacteria themselves NEVER cross the epithelium or enter the bloodstream. Cholera is a pure **toxin-mediated (non-invasive)** disease. All damage is done by the **CTXφ bacteriophage-encoded** cholera toxin.

## 2.2 Stage-Wise Pathogenesis of Typhoid (*Salmonella Typhi*)

Unlike cholera, typhoid is an **invasive, intracellular, systemic disease**:

### Stage 1: Ingestion and Survival
- *S. Typhi* is ingested via contaminated water/food.
- The bacteria are uniquely adapted to survive **inside macrophages** via the **Vi capsular antigen** (a polysaccharide capsule exclusive to *S. Typhi*).

### Stage 2: M-Cell Transcytosis and Mucosal Invasion
- *S. Typhi* specifically targets the **M (microfold) cells** overlying Peyer's patches in the ileum.
- Using the **Type III Secretion System (T3SS)** encoded on **Salmonella Pathogenicity Island 1 (SPI-1)**, it injects effector proteins (SipA, SipC, SopE, SopB) into the M-cell.
- This triggers **actin cytoskeleton rearrangement** → **membrane ruffling** → bacterial engulfment.

### Stage 3: Intracellular Replication in Macrophages
- Inside macrophages, *S. Typhi* resides within a unique **Salmonella-Containing Vacuole (SCV)**.
- It expresses **SPI-2 effectors** (SseF, SseG, SifA) that prevent phagolysosome fusion and resist reactive oxygen species.

### Stage 4: Dissemination via Reticuloendothelial System
- Infected macrophages carry the bacteria via the **lymphatics → bloodstream** (primary bacteremia).
- Bacteria seed the **liver, spleen, gallbladder, and bone marrow** — the classic sites of typhoid persistence.

### Stage 5: Secondary Bacteremia and Organ Damage
- Bacteria re-enter the bloodstream from these organs (secondary bacteremia) → sustained fever (39–40°C), **rose spots** on the abdomen, hepatosplenomegaly, and **pea-soup diarrhea**.
- Gallbladder colonization creates **chronic asymptomatic carriers** (e.g., "Typhoid Mary" — Mary Mallon, who infected 51 people in early 20th century USA).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Contrast for KTU 2024:** Cholera is **toxin-mediated, non-invasive, intestinal-only**. Typhoid is **invasive, intracellular, systemic**. This single comparison fetches 7 marks in a 14-mark question.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Parameter Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Cholera | Typhoid | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Causative agent | *Vibrio cholerae* | *Salmonella enterica* ser. *Typhi* | Gram-negative rods |
| Key toxin / Virulence | Cholera Toxin (CT), AB₅ exotoxin | Vi capsular antigen, SPI-1/SPI-2 T3SS | CTXφ phage encoded |
| Molecular target | Gsα subunit (ADP-ribosylation) | Actin cytoskeleton, SCV formation | GTPase modification |
| Second messenger | cAMP ↑↑ | Calcium + actin rearrangement | Signalling cascade |
| Ion channel | CFTR (Cl⁻ efflux) | NHE3 (Na⁺/H⁺ exchange) | Apical membrane |
| Net fluid movement | Secretory (efflux) | Mixed (invasive inflammation) | Hypotonic loss |
| Invasiveness | Non-invasive | Highly invasive | M-cell entry |
| Incubation period | 2 hrs – 5 days | 6 – 30 days | Time to symptoms |
| ID₅₀ | 10⁶ – 10⁸ CFU | 10⁵ – 10⁶ CFU | Colony Forming Units |
| R₀ (unvaccinated) | 2 – 6 (up to 10+) | 1.3 – 3.0 | Reproductive number |
| Carrier state | Short-term (weeks) | Chronic (years – gallbladder) | Public health impact |
| Diagnostic test | Stool dark-field microscopy, dipstick | Blood culture (week 1), Widal test | Gold standard varies |
| Vaccine available | Yes (CVD 103-HgR, Dukoral) | Yes (Ty21a, Vi polysaccharide) | WHO prequalified |
| Specific antibiotic | Doxycycline, Azithromycin | Ciprofloxacin, Ceftriaxone | Resistance emerging |
| Rehydration therapy | **Oral Rehydration Salts (ORS)** is cornerstone | Supportive, not primary | Na⁺/glucose cotransport |

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a B.Tech student must study pathogenesis:** This is not just a Biology question. The R₀ value, the cAMP-driven CFTR chloride flux, and the bacterial load calculations are directly used in:
> - **Environmental Engineering:** Designing municipal water treatment plants (chlorination dose, filtration efficacy).
> - **Biomedical Engineering:** Engineering oral rehydration therapy formulations (Na⁺/glucose molar ratios exploit the SGLT1 cotransporter to overcome CFTR-mediated Cl⁻ loss).
> - **Public Health Engineering:** Modeling outbreak dynamics, designing sewage epidemiology, and building sanitation infrastructure.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Molecular Derivation: Cholera Toxin ADP-Ribosylation Cascade

The biochemical cascade from CT binding to fluid loss is fully derivable for KTU 14-mark answers. The full molecular mechanism is presented step-by-step below.

**Step 1: Toxin B-Subunit Binding to GM1 Ganglioside**

$$ \text{CT (AB}_5\text{)} + \text{GM1}_{\text{membrane}} \longrightarrow \text{CT}_{\text{bound}} $$

**Step 2: A-Subunit Endocytosis and Activation**

The active A1 fragment is generated by proteolytic nicking:

$$ \text{A} \xrightarrow{\text{trypsin/protease}} \text{A1} + \text{A2} $$

**Step 3: ADP-Ribosylation of Gsα (The Core Pathogenic Reaction)**

The A1 fragment catalyzes the transfer of ADP-ribose from NAD⁺ to a specific arginine residue (Arg-201) on the Gsα subunit:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NAD}^+ + \text{Gs}\alpha_{\text{inactive (GDP-bound)}} &\xrightarrow{\text{A1 fragment}} \\
\text{ADP-ribose-Gs}\alpha_{\text{locked active}} &+ \text{nicotinamide} + \text{H}^+
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Loss of GTPase Activity**

Normally, Gsα hydrolyzes GTP → GDP to self-terminate signalling. The modification at Arg-201 **blocks intrinsic GTPase activity**:

$$ \text{ADP-ribose-Gs}\alpha\text{-GTP} \xrightarrow{\text{X}} \text{ADP-ribose-Gs}\alpha\text{-GDP} \quad (\text{reaction blocked}) $$

**Step 5: Constitutive Adenylate Cyclase Activation**

Locked-active Gsα permanently stimulates Adenylate Cyclase (AC):

$$ \text{ATP} \xrightarrow{\text{AC (constitutively active)}} \text{cAMP} + \text{PP}_i $$

**Step 6: cAMP Accumulation Drives CFTR Opening**

The elevated intracellular cAMP activates Protein Kinase A (PKA), which phosphorylates the CFTR chloride channel on the apical membrane:

$$ \text{CFTR}_{\text{closed}} \xrightarrow{\text{PKA phosphorylation}} \text{CFTR}_{\text{open}} $$

**Step 7: Chloride Efflux and Osmotic Water Loss**

Chloride flows down its electrochemical gradient into the lumen:

$$ \text{Cl}^-_{\text{cytoplasm}} \longrightarrow \text{Cl}^-_{\text{lumen}} $$

By electroneutrality and osmotic balance, Na⁺ and H₂O follow:

$$ \text{Na}^+_{\text{cytoplasm}} \longrightarrow \text{Na}^+_{\text{lumen}}, \quad \text{H}_2\text{O}_{\text{body}} \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}_{\text{lumen}} $$

**Net Result: Massive Secretory Diarrhea (Rice-Water Stools)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The logical chain for KTU 2024 14-mark answer must include the full chain:**
> CT → GM1 binding → A1 fragment → NAD⁺-dependent ADP-ribosylation of Gsα (Arg-201) → GTPase inhibition → permanent AC activation → cAMP↑ → PKA activation → CFTR phosphorylation → Cl⁻ efflux → Na⁺/H₂O follow → dehydration.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Pathogenesis Table

This **comparative matrix** is the single most important table for KTU 14-mark questions asking "Differentiate the pathogenesis of Cholera and Typhoid":

| Step | Cholera Pathogenesis | Typhoid Pathogenesis |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Portal of entry | Mouth → gastric acid survival | Mouth → gastric acid survival |
| 2. Site of action | Small intestine (jejunum, ileum) lumen | Ileum (Peyer's patches) M cells |
| 3. Adhesion mechanism | ToxR-regulated pili (TCP) | T3SS + SPI-1 effectors (SipA, SopE) |
| 4. Type of damage | **Toxin-mediated (extracellular)** | **Invasion-mediated (intracellular)** |
| 5. Key virulence | Cholera toxin (AB₅ exotoxin) | Vi antigen + SPI-1/SPI-2 T3SS |
| 6. Cellular entry | Does NOT enter enterocytes | Actively invades via M cells |
| 7. Molecular target | Gsα (ADP-ribosylation) | Actin cytoskeleton, SCV formation |
| 8. Host response | Minimal inflammation | Granulomatous inflammation, ulceration |
| 9. Systemic spread | **None** — purely intestinal | Primary & secondary bacteremia |
| 10. Organs involved | Gut only | Liver, spleen, gallbladder, bone marrow |
| 11. Clinical hallmark | **Rice-water stools**, no fever | **Step-ladder fever**, rose spots, hepatosplenomegaly |
| 12. Stool character | Watery, colorless, with mucus flecks | Pea-soup green diarrhea |
| 13. Key treatment | **Oral Rehydration Salts (ORS)** | **Ciprofloxacin / Ceftriaxone** |
| 14. Carrier state | Short-term, intestinal | **Chronic gallbladder carriers** (Typhoid Mary) |
| 15. Fatality (untreated) | 25–50% | 10–30% |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Fluid Loss Calculation in Cholera

A 60 kg adult cholera patient loses water at the rate of **1 liter per hour**. Calculate the dehydration percentage over 8 hours, and determine the required ORS volume (assume 75 mL/kg deficit replacement in first 4 hours).

**Given:**
- Patient body weight: $W = 60 \text{ kg}$
- Fluid loss rate: $Q_{\text{loss}} = 1 \text{ L/hr}$
- Time elapsed: $t = 8 \text{ hr}$

**Step 1: Total fluid lost in 8 hours**

$$ V_{\text{lost}} = Q_{\text{loss}} \times t = 1 \text{ L/hr} \times 8 \text{ hr} = 8 \text{ L} $$

**Step 2: Convert to body weight basis**

$$ \text{Loss per kg} = \frac{V_{\text{lost}}}{W} = \frac{8 \text{ L}}{60 \text{ kg}} = 0.133 \text{ L/kg} = 133 \text{ mL/kg} $$

**Step 3: Classify dehydration severity (WHO standard)**

| Severity | Fluid loss (% body weight) | mL/kg lost |
|---|---|---|
| No dehydration | < 5% | < 50 |
| **Some dehydration** | 5 – 10% | 50 – 100 |
| **Severe dehydration** | > 10% | > 100 |

> Result: **133 mL/kg = 13.3% loss → SEVERE DEHYDRATION → hypovolemic shock risk imminent.**

**Step 4: ORS volume required in first 4 hours (WHO Plan C)**

$$ V_{\text{ORS}} = 75 \text{ mL/kg} \times 60 \text{ kg} = 4500 \text{ mL} = 4.5 \text{ L} $$

**Step 5: Hourly rate over first 4 hours**

$$ R_{\text{hourly}} = \frac{4500 \text{ mL}}{4 \text{ hr}} = 1125 \text{ mL/hr} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering takeaway:** The patient is losing water faster than ORS can be administered → IV Ringer's Lactate must be started immediately. This is why cholera is a medical emergency and not just a "stomach bug."

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: Basic Reproduction Number (R₀) Comparison

The basic reproduction number R₀ represents the average number of secondary infections produced by one infected individual in a fully susceptible population. For water-borne diseases, R₀ is strongly affected by sanitation infrastructure (S).

**Simplified R₀ model for water-borne diseases:**

$$ R_0 = \frac{\beta \cdot c \cdot d}{S} $$

Where:
- $\beta$ = transmission probability per contact
- $c$ = contact rate with contaminated water
- $d$ = duration of infectiousness (days)
- $S$ = sanitation effectiveness (0 = no sanitation, 1 = perfect sanitation)

**Comparison for a Kerala village (S = 0.3, poor sanitation):**

| Disease | $\beta$ | $c$ (contacts/day) | $d$ (days) | $S$ | Calculated $R_0$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cholera | 0.5 | 3 | 5 | 0.3 | $\frac{0.5 \times 3 \times 5}{0.3} = 25$ |
| Typhoid | 0.3 | 2 | 21 | 0.3 | $\frac{0.3 \times 2 \times 21}{0.3} = 42$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** In a poorly sanitized village, *both* diseases will cause explosive outbreaks. Typhoid has a higher R₀ because of the long infectious duration (chronic carriers). Improving sanitation (S → 1) would reduce both R₀ values to 7.5 and 12.6 respectively, demonstrating why **infrastructure engineering** is more important than drugs for long-term control.
