# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q1]**
> **Q1.** Define the term "Infective Dose 50" (ID₅₀). Mention the ID₅₀ values for *V. cholerae* and *S. Typhi*.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The Infective Dose 50 (ID₅₀) is defined as the number of viable microbial cells required to produce a demonstrable infection in 50% of a test population under standardized conditions. It is a quantitative measure of pathogen virulence.
> - ID₅₀ of *Vibrio cholerae* = **10⁶ to 10⁸ CFU** (higher dose required due to acid sensitivity).
> - ID₅₀ of *Salmonella Typhi* = **10⁵ to 10⁶ CFU** (lower dose due to acid tolerance and Vi capsule).
> *Examiner's note: Mentioning units as CFU and giving the correct order of magnitude fetches full marks.*

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q2]**
> **Q2.** Why is ORS (Oral Rehydration Salts) effective in cholera even though the disease causes massive fluid loss?
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> ORS exploits the **SGLT1 (Sodium-Glucose Linked Transporter)** on the intestinal epithelial cells. Even when the CFTR channel is open and Cl⁻ is being lost, the SGLT1 co-transporter actively absorbs **Na⁺ along with glucose** in a 1:1 molar ratio. Water follows osmotically. Therefore, ORS (which contains NaCl + glucose in correct ratio) **replaces the lost fluid** through the still-functional SGLT1 pathway, bypassing the cholera toxin blockade. *(1 mark for naming SGLT1, 1 mark for mechanism, 1 mark for clinical implication.)*

## 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4, Set A]**

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Cholera Pathogenesis**

**(a)** Describe in detail the pathogenesis of cholera caused by *Vibrio cholerae*. Add a note on the molecular mechanism of cholera toxin. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain why cholera is classified as a non-invasive toxin-mediated disease, and discuss the engineering basis of oral rehydration therapy. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Question A**

**(a) Pathogenesis of Cholera – 7 marks:**

1. **Ingestion and Survival (1 mark):** *V. cholerae* is ingested via contaminated water. The bacteria are motile (single polar flagellum) and survive gastric acid at high doses (ID₅₀ = 10⁶–10⁸).

2. **Colonization of Small Intestine (1 mark):** In the alkaline environment of the duodenum/jejunum, the **ToxR regulon** is activated. Bacteria produce the **Toxin-Coregulated Pilus (TCP)** which allows microcolony formation on the brush border of enterocytes.

3. **Toxin Secretion (1 mark):** Bacteria release **Cholera Toxin (CT)**, an AB₅ exotoxin encoded by the **CTXφ bacteriophage**. The five B subunits bind **GM1 ganglioside** on the host cell membrane.

4. **A-Subunit Entry and ADP-Ribosylation (2 marks):** The A1 fragment enters the cell and catalyzes the transfer of ADP-ribose from NAD⁺ to **Arg-201 of the Gsα subunit** of the heterotrimeric G-protein. This modification **blocks the intrinsic GTPase activity** of Gsα, locking it in the active GTP-bound state.

5. **cAMP Accumulation and Fluid Efflux (1 mark):** Permanently active Gsα → constitutive activation of **Adenylate Cyclase** → massive **cAMP** rise → activation of **PKA** → phosphorylation of **CFTR** channel → **Cl⁻ efflux** into the lumen → **Na⁺ and H₂O follow** → **rice-water stools**.

6. **Clinical Outcome (1 mark):** Severe isotonic dehydration leading to hypovolemic shock, metabolic acidosis, and death within hours if untreated. The bacteria themselves **never invade** the epithelium.

**(b) Non-Invasive Nature and ORS Engineering Basis – 7 marks:**

1. **Evidence of Non-Invasiveness (2 marks):** Cholera bacteria remain confined to the intestinal lumen. No bacteremia, no mucosal invasion, no inflammatory infiltrate. All damage is mediated by extracellular cholera toxin. Stool contains no leukocytes or blood.

2. **Why Non-Invasive Yet Severe (2 marks):** The **massive cAMP-driven Cl⁻ efflux** overwhelms the colon's absorptive capacity, leading to isotonic fluid loss. The **toxin acts catalytically** — picomolar quantities can produce liters of fluid.

3. **Engineering Basis of ORS (3 marks):**
   - **Principle:** ORS exploits the **SGLT1 co-transporter**, which is unaffected by cholera toxin.
   - **Composition:** Standard WHO-ORS = **NaCl 75 mmol/L + glucose 75 mmol/L + KCl 20 mmol/L + trisodium citrate 10 mmol/L**.
   - **Mechanism:** SGLT1 co-transports **1 Na⁺ + 1 glucose** molecule per cycle → creates osmotic gradient → water absorption follows.
   - **Design equation:** Na⁺:glucose molar ratio of **1:1** is critical for maximum absorption.
   - **Outcome:** ORS replaces fluid loss at **~30% lower cost than IV fluids** and is field-deployable, revolutionizing cholera management.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4, Set B]**

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Typhoid Pathogenesis**

**(a)** Describe the pathogenesis of typhoid fever with a clear focus on the role of Salmonella Pathogenicity Islands (SPI-1 and SPI-2) and the Vi antigen. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast the pathogenesis of typhoid and cholera. Explain the concept of chronic carriers with reference to the historical case of "Typhoid Mary." **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Question B**

**(a) Pathogenesis of Typhoid – 7 marks:**

1. **Ingestion and Survival (1 mark):** *S. Typhi* is ingested via contaminated water. The **Vi capsular antigen** (a polysaccharide capsule) protects the bacterium from complement-mediated killing and phagocytosis in the gut lumen.

2. **M-Cell Targeting and Invasion (2 marks):** *S. Typhi* binds to **M (microfold) cells** overlying Peyer's patches in the terminal ileum. The **SPI-1 Type III Secretion System** acts as a molecular syringe, injecting effectors (SipA, SipC, SopE, SopB) that cause **actin rearrangement, membrane ruffling**, and engulfment.

3. **Macrophage Survival (2 marks):** After transcytosis, bacteria are phagocytosed by subepithelial macrophages. Inside, they reside in the **Salmonella-Containing Vacuole (SCV)**. The **SPI-2 T3SS** injects effectors (SseF, SseG, SifA, PipB2) that **prevent phagolysosome fusion**, inhibit NADPH oxidase, and allow intracellular replication.

4. **Systemic Dissemination (1 mark):** Infected macrophages migrate via lymphatics to **mesenteric lymph nodes → bloodstream (primary bacteremia) → liver, spleen, gallbladder, bone marrow**. Secondary bacteremia from these RES organs causes the **step-ladder fever, rose spots, and hepatosplenomegaly**.

5. **Chronic Carrier State (1 mark):** **Gallbladder colonization** (often in gallstones) creates chronic asymptomatic carriers who shed bacteria for years.

**(b) Comparison and Typhoid Mary – 7 marks:**

1. **Comparison Table (3 marks):** Present the comparative table from Section 3.2 with at least 8 distinct points.

2. **Key Conceptual Differences (2 marks):**
   - Cholera: **extracellular toxin strategy**, no invasion, no inflammation, no fever.
   - Typhoid: **intracellular invasion strategy**, systemic spread, granulomatous inflammation, sustained fever.

3. **Typhoid Mary Case Study (2 marks):**
   - **Mary Mallon (1869–1938)** was an Irish-American cook who worked for affluent New York families.
   - She was a **chronic asymptomatic gallbladder carrier** of *S. Typhi*, shedding ~10⁹ bacteria per gram of stool.
   - She is credited with **51 cases of typhoid** and 3 deaths, leading to her forced quarantine twice on North Brother Island.
   - **Public health lesson:** Chronic carriers must be identified, restricted from food-handling occupations, and treated (cholecystectomy in persistent cases). This case directly motivated the development of **carrier-state screening** in modern epidemiology.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not confuse *S. Typhi* with *S. Typhimurium*.** *Typhi* is the strict human pathogen causing typhoid; *Typhimurium* causes gastroenteritis in many species. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 2. **Do not write "cholera bacteria enter the blood."** They do not. Cholera is **non-invasive**. Lose 2 marks if you say so.
> 3. **Do not omit the Gsα / ADP-ribosylation step** in cholera pathogenesis. It is worth 2 marks and is the molecular core of the disease.
> 4. **Do not write SPI-1 and SPI-2 as the same system.** SPI-1 is for **invasion** (extracellular), SPI-2 is for **intracellular survival** (after phagocytosis). Examiners reward this distinction.
> 5. **Do not forget "rice-water stools"** as the pathognomonic feature of cholera. It is the single most important clinical buzzword.
> 6. **Units matter:** Always write "CFU" (Colony Forming Units), not just "10⁶ bacteria," in ID₅₀ answers.
> 7. **Typhoid Mary** is a frequently asked 2-mark sub-question. If asked "Give an example of a chronic carrier," she is the textbook answer.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU 2024 Prep):**
> - **Cholera agent:** *V. cholerae* O1/O139; **Typhoid agent:** *S. enterica* ser. *Typhi*.
> - **Cholera key words:** AB₅ toxin, GM1 ganglioside, Gsα ADP-ribosylation, cAMP↑, CFTR, rice-water stools, ORS/SGLT1, non-invasive.
> - **Typhoid key words:** Vi capsular antigen, M cells, SPI-1 (invasion), SPI-2 (intracellular survival), SCV, RES seeding, step-ladder fever, rose spots, chronic gallbladder carrier.
> - **ID₅₀ values:** Cholera 10⁶–10⁸ CFU; Typhoid 10⁵–10⁶ CFU.
> - **Incubation periods:** Cholera 2 hrs – 5 days; Typhoid 6 – 30 days.
> - **Comparative strategy:** Cholera = toxin-mediated extracellular; Typhoid = invasion-mediated intracellular.
> - **Treatment pillars:** Cholera → ORS (rehydration); Typhoid → Antibiotics (ciprofloxacin, ceftriaxone).
> - **Public health engineering:** Sanitation infrastructure (S) directly reduces R₀; clean water supply is more cost-effective than drug treatment at the population level.
> - **Historical figure:** Typhoid Mary (Mary Mallon) — chronic gallbladder carrier.
> - **Vaccines:** Dukoral/CVD 103-HgR for cholera; Ty21a/Vi polysaccharide for typhoid.
> - **Final clinical buzzwords:** Rice-water stools (cholera), pea-soup diarrhea (typhoid), rose spots (typhoid), step-ladder fever (typhoid), no fever (cholera).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Food Microbiology: Bacteria and Fungi Causing Food Spoilage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT303 - ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Food Microbiology: Bacteria and Fungi Causing Food Spoilage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4: FOOD MICROBIOLOGY – BACTERIA AND FUNGI CAUSING FOOD SPOILAGE

## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **Food Spoilage** is defined as any undesirable change in the sensory, nutritional, or safety attributes of a food product, occurring due to the metabolic activity of microorganisms (bacteria, yeasts, and molds), endogenous enzymes, or chemical reactions, which renders the food unacceptable for human consumption.

According to the **International Commission on Microbiological Specifications for Foods (ICMSF)**, a food is considered spoiled when it becomes unfit for consumption due to:
- Visible microbial growth (molds, slime, colonies)
- Off-odors and off-flavors (sour, putrid, rancid)
- Texture changes (gas, slime, liquefaction)
- Toxic metabolite accumulation (mycotoxins, biogenic amines)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE] 
> **Analogy: The Food Battlefield** 🍎⚔️🦠
> 
> Imagine your food as a **fortified city**:
> - **Food** = The city, rich in resources (water, sugars, proteins, fats)
> - **Microorganisms** = Invading armies with different weapons and strategies
> - **Preservation methods** = The city's defensive walls (refrigeration, salt, packaging)
> - **Storage conditions** = The weather that either weakens the walls or strengthens the invaders
> 
> When the **defensive walls** (cold chain, low pH, low water activity) collapse, the **invading armies** (bacteria, fungi) march in, consume the city's resources (nutrients), and leave behind waste products (organic acids, sulfides, amines, gases). This is precisely what we call **food spoilage** — the city has been "conquered."

### 1.3 Key Microbial Targets in Food Spoilage

| Food Constituent | Primary Spoilage Agents | End Products |
|---|---|---|
| Proteins | Bacteria (Clostridium, Pseudomonas) | Amines, H₂S, indole, putrescine |
| Carbohydrates | Bacteria, Yeasts | Organic acids, CO₂, ethanol |
| Lipids | Bacteria, Molds | Fatty acids, peroxides (rancidity) |
| Pectins | Bacteria (Erwinia) | Soft rot, tissue maceration |

### 1.4 The Big Picture – Spoilage Triangle

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **The Spoilage Triangle (Three Factors Must Intersect):**
> 
> 1. **Substrate** (suitable food with nutrients)
> 2. **Microorganism** (spoilage agent with right enzymes)
> 3. **Environment** (pH, aw, T, O₂ favouring growth)
> 
> Eliminate ANY ONE of these three factors → Food Preservation Achieved.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD REFERENCE

## 2.1 Classification of Food Spoilage Microorganisms

### 2.1.1 Bacterial Spoilage Agents – Detailed Analysis

**A. Pseudomonas spp.**
- Morphology: Gram-negative, aerobic, motile rods
- Optimal Temperature: **20–30 °C (psychrotrophs can grow at 4 °C)**
- Target Foods: Meat, poultry, fish, milk, eggs
- Spoilage Type: Produces **slimy, pigmented colonies** (pyocyanin blue-green), putrefactive odors from protein breakdown
- Key Enzymes: Lipases, proteases, lecithinases
- **KTU Highlight:** Major cause of **psychrotrophic spoilage** in refrigerated foods

**B. Lactobacillus spp.**
- Morphology: Gram-positive, non-spore forming rods, facultative anaerobes
- Optimal pH: **3.5–6.8** (acid-tolerant)
- Target Foods: Milk, cheese, wines, pickled vegetables, meat
- Spoilage Type: **Souring** – produces lactic acid, acetic acid; causes flat sour spoilage in canned tomatoes/vegetables

**C. Bacillus spp.**
- Morphology: Gram-positive, aerobic/facultative, **spore-forming rods**
- Notable Species: *B. cereus*, *B. subtilis*, *B. licheniformis*
- Target Foods: Rice, pasta, bread (ropy bread), canned foods
- Spoilage Type: **Ropy/spongy texture** due to levan/dextrose capsule formation
- **KTU Highlight:** Spores survive extreme heat – critical for canned food failure (flat sour spoilage)

**D. Clostridium spp.**
- Morphology: Gram-positive, **obligate anaerobes**, spore-forming rods
- Notable Species: *C. botulinum* (botulism), *C. perfringens* (gas gangrene), *C. sporogenes*
- Target Foods: Canned meat, vacuum-packed fish, vacuum-sealed meat
- Spoilage Type: **Putrefaction** – produces H₂S, NH₃, foul-smelling amines, gas (CO₂, H₂)
- **Critical:** *C. botulinum* produces **botulinum toxin** – one of the most lethal neurotoxins (LD₅₀ ≈ 1–2 μg orally)

**E. Staphylococcus aureus**
- Morphology: Gram-positive cocci in clusters, facultative anaerobe
- Target Foods: Custards, ham, salads, dairy products
- Spoilage/Disease: Produces **heat-stable enterotoxins** (SEA, SEB, SEC) → gastroenteritis
- **KTU Highlight:** Salt-tolerant (grows up to 15% NaCl) – survives in cured meats

**F. Salmonella spp.**
- Gram-negative, facultatively anaerobic rods
- Target: Poultry, eggs, dairy, seafood
- Pathology: Produces endotoxin → typhoid, gastroenteritis
- **Critical:** Heat-labile – killed by proper cooking (70 °C for 2 min)

**G. Erwinia carotovora (Pectobacterium)**
- Gram-negative, pectinolytic
- Target: Potatoes, carrots, vegetables
- Spoilage: **Soft rot** – produces pectinases that dissolve plant cell walls → mushy, watery tissue

**H. Listeria monocytogenes**
- Gram-positive, non-spore-forming rod
- Unique Feature: Grows at **0–45 °C** (most cold-tolerant pathogen)
- Target: Soft cheeses, deli meats, unpasteurized milk
- **KTU Highlight:** Major concern for refrigerated ready-to-eat (RTE) foods

---

### 2.1.2 Fungal Spoilage Agents (Molds and Yeasts) – Detailed Analysis

**A. Aspergillus spp.**
- Common Species: *A. niger*, *A. flavus*, *A. parasiticus*, *A. fumigatus*
- Target Foods: Grains, peanuts, spices, fruits, bread
- Spoilage: Black/green/yellow mold growth
- **CRITICAL TOXIN:** *A. flavus* and *A. parasiticus* produce **Aflatoxin B₁** (most potent natural hepatocarcinogen – IARC Group 1)
- Optimal Conditions: **aw = 0.85–0.95; T = 25–35 °C**

**B. Penicillium spp.**
- Common Species: *P. roqueforti*, *P. camemberti*, *P. expansum*
- Target Foods: Citrus fruits, apples, cheese, bread
- Spoilage: Blue-green molds (intentional in Roquefort cheese)
- Toxins: *P. citrinum* produces **citrinin** (nephrotoxic)
- *P. expansum* produces **patulin** (carcinogenic, found in rotten apples)

**C. Rhizopus spp. (Black Bread Mold)**
- Zygomycete, fast-growing
- Target: Bread, soft fruits, vegetables
- Spoilage: Coarse black mycelium on bread surfaces

**D. Mucor spp.**
- Zygomycete, white fluffy growth
- Target: Vegetables, cheese, refrigerated foods
- Spoilage: Cottony, white to grayish mold

**E. Botrytis cinerea (Gray Mold)**
- Target: Grapes, strawberries, lettuce
- Spoilage: Gray fuzzy mold with extensive tissue maceration

**F. Fusarium spp.**
- Target: Cereals (maize, wheat, barley)
- Toxins: **Trichothecenes** (T-2 toxin), **Zearalenone**, **Fumonisins** (cause esophageal cancer)
- Optimal: Cool, moist conditions

**G. Alternaria spp.**
- Target: Tomatoes, citrus, grains
- Toxins: **Alternariol**, **Alternariol monomethyl ether**

**H. Geotrichum candidum**
- Yeast-like mold
- Target: Dairy, cheese, fruits
- Spoilage: Cream-colored, velvety growth

**I. Saccharomyces spp. (Wild Yeasts)**
- Target: Wines, juices, syrups, honey
- Spoilage: Unwanted fermentation → CO₂, ethanol, off-flavors

---

## 2.2 Factors Influencing Microbial Spoilage (Intrinsic & Extrinsic)

### 2.2.1 Intrinsic Factors (Food-Related)

| Factor | Definition | Threshold/Optimum | Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| **pH** | $-\log[\text{H}^+]$ | Most bacteria: 6.5–7.5; Molds/yeasts: 4.5–6.0 | Acidic pH inhibits bacteria, favours fungi |
| **Water Activity (aw)** | Ratio of vapour pressure of food to pure water | Bacteria: $\geq 0.91$; Yeasts: $\geq 0.88$; Molds: $\geq 0.80$; Halophiles: as low as $0.75$ | Controls microbial growth availability of water |
| **Redox Potential (Eh)** | Electron availability | Aerobes need positive Eh; Anaerobes need negative Eh | Determines aerobic/anaerobic spoilage |
| **Nutrient Content** | Proteins, carbs, fats, vitamins | High protein → bacterial putrefaction; High sugar → fungal/osmophilic spoilage | Substrate determines which organism thrives |
| **Antimicrobial Components** | Lysozyme, lactoferrin, lactoperoxidase in milk; eugenol in spices | Variable | Natural preservation |

### 2.2.2 Extrinsic Factors (Storage-Related)

- **Temperature**: Refrigeration (0–4 °C), Freezing ($-18$ °C), Ambient (25 °C)
- **Relative Humidity (RH)**: High RH → mold growth on surfaces
- **Atmosphere (O₂/CO₂)**: Vacuum packaging, Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP)

---

## 2.3 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA & PARAMETER SHEET

| Parameter | Symbol | Formula/Expression | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Water Activity | $a_w$ | $a_w = \dfrac{P_{food}}{P_{pure\,water}} = \dfrac{RH_{equilibrium}}{100}$ | $0$ to $1$ (pure water $= 1$) | Dimensionless |
| pH | pH | $\text{pH} = -\log_{10}[\text{H}^+]$ | $0$ to $14$ | Dimensionless |
| Generation Time | $g$ | $g = \dfrac{t}{n}$ where $n = \dfrac{\log N - \log N_0}{\log 2}$ | Bacteria: $20$ min – $2$ hr | Minutes/Hours |
| Decimal Reduction Time | $D$ | Time at constant T to kill 90% of cells | $D_{121}$ for *C. botulinum* $\approx 0.21$ min | Minutes |
| Thermal Death Time | $TDT$ | Time to kill entire population at constant T | Variable | Minutes |
| Z-value | $z$ | Temperature increase needed to reduce $D$ by 90% | *C. botulinum*: $z = 10\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ | °C |
| Specific Growth Rate | $\mu$ | $\mu = \dfrac{\ln N - \ln N_0}{t}$ | Variable | hr$^{-1}$ |
| Lag Phase Duration | $L$ | Empirical; depends on inoculum history | Hours | Hours |
| F-value (Sterilization) | $F_0$ | Equivalent sterilization time at $121.1\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ | $F_0 = \sum 10^{\frac{T-121.1}{z}} \cdot \Delta t$ | Minutes |

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **Critical Threshold Values to Memorize:**
> - Minimum $a_w$ for bacterial growth: **0.91**
> - Minimum $a_w$ for yeast growth: **0.88**
> - Minimum $a_w$ for mold growth: **0.80**
> - pH range for most food-borne pathogens: **4.5 – 9.5**
> - Optimum temperature for psychrotrophs: **3 – 7 °C**
> - Optimum temperature for mesophiles: **20 – 45 °C**

---

## 2.4 Spoilage Mechanisms – Chemical Basis

### 2.4.1 Protein Spoilage (Putrefaction)
$$\text{Proteins} \xrightarrow{\text{Proteases}} \text{Peptides} \xrightarrow{\text{Peptidases}} \text{Amino Acids} \xrightarrow{\text{Decarboxylase}} \text{Biogenic Amines} + \text{CO}_2$$

Biogenic amines produced: **Cadaverine, Putrescine, Histamine, Tyramine** (indicators of spoilage).

### 2.4.2 Carbohydrate Spoilage (Fermentation)
$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \xrightarrow{\text{Glycolysis}} 2\,\text{Pyruvate} \rightarrow \text{Lactic Acid / Acetic Acid / CO}_2 + \text{Ethanol}$$

### 2.4.3 Lipid Spoilage (Rancidity)
- **Hydrolytic rancidity**: Lipases → Free fatty acids → Soapy, bitter taste
- **Oxidative rancidity**: Unsaturated fatty acids + O₂ → Peroxides → Aldehydes, ketones → Off-odors

### 2.4.4 Pectin Degradation (Soft Rot)
$$\text{Protopectin} \xrightarrow{\text{Pectinase (Erwinia, Botrytis)}} \text{Soluble Pectin} \rightarrow \text{Tissue Maceration}$$
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP ANALYTICAL MATRIX & DERIVATIONS

## 3.1 Determination of Water Activity (aw) – Step-by-Step Derivation

The water activity is a critical parameter for predicting microbial growth. Its derivation proceeds from thermodynamic principles:

### Step 1: Definition from Vapour Pressure
Water activity is defined as the **ratio of the partial vapour pressure of water in the food substrate to that of pure water at the same temperature**:
$$a_w = \dfrac{P_{solution}}{P_{water}^{\circ}}$$

where:
- $P_{solution}$ = vapour pressure of water over the food
- $P_{water}^{\circ}$ = vapour pressure of pure water at temperature $T$

### Step 2: Relation to Equilibrium Relative Humidity
At equilibrium, the relative humidity of air in contact with the food equals the $a_w$ (expressed as a percentage):
$$a_w = \dfrac{ERH}{100}$$
where $ERH$ is the **Equilibrium Relative Humidity** (%).

### Step 3: Relation to Raoult's Law
For an ideal dilute solution:
$$P_{solution} = X_w \cdot P_{water}^{\circ}$$
where $X_w$ = mole fraction of water.

Substituting:
$$a_w = X_w \approx \dfrac{n_w}{n_w + n_s}$$
where $n_w$ = moles of water, $n_s$ = moles of solute.

### Step 4: Bound vs. Free Water
In real foods, water is partially bound to proteins, starches, and salts. Only **free water** is available for microbial reactions, so $a_w$ is typically lower than $X_w$.

### Worked Example
**Problem:** A food sample has $X_w = 0.985$. Predict the lowest microbial growth possible.
- $a_w \approx 0.985$ (ideal)
- Since $0.985 > 0.91$ → supports **all bacteria, yeasts, and molds**

---

## 3.2 Microbial Growth Curve – Analytical Derivation

### Step 1: Exponential Growth Equation
During the exponential phase, microbial population doubles at regular intervals:
$$N = N_0 \cdot 2^{n}$$
where $n$ = number of generations, $N_0$ = initial population, $N$ = final population.

### Step 2: Number of Generations
Taking logarithm (base 2):
$$n = \dfrac{\log N - \log N_0}{\log 2} = \dfrac{\log N - \log N_0}{0.301}$$

### Step 3: Generation Time
$$g = \dfrac{t}{n} = \dfrac{t \cdot \log 2}{\log N - \log N_0}$$

### Step 4: Specific Growth Rate
$$\mu = \dfrac{\ln 2}{g} = \dfrac{0.693}{g}$$

### Worked Example
**Problem:** A bacterial population grows from $10^3$ CFU/mL to $10^8$ CFU/mL in 6 hours. Calculate generation time and specific growth rate.

**Solution:**

Step 1 – Calculate number of generations:
$$n = \dfrac{\log(10^8) - \log(10^3)}{0.301} = \dfrac{8 - 3}{0.301} = \dfrac{5}{0.301} = 16.61 \text{ generations}$$

Step 2 – Calculate generation time:
$$g = \dfrac{t}{n} = \dfrac{360 \text{ min}}{16.61} = 21.67 \text{ min/generation}$$

Step 3 – Calculate specific growth rate:
$$\mu = \dfrac{0.693}{21.67} = 0.0319 \text{ min}^{-1} = 1.91 \text{ hr}^{-1}$$

> [!NOTE] 
> **Interpretation:** Each cell divides every ~22 minutes — typical of *E. coli* or *Pseudomonas* under optimal conditions.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix – Microbial Growth vs. Intrinsic Parameters

| Food Type | pH | $a_w$ | Expected Spoilage Organism | Spoilage Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Fresh meat (5–6) | 5.5–6.5 | 0.99 | *Pseudomonas* | Slimy, putrid |
| Fresh fish (5.5–6.5) | 6.0–6.5 | 0.98 | *Pseudomonas*, *Shewanella* | TMA odor, slime |
| Milk (6.5) | 6.5–6.7 | 0.97 | *Lactobacillus*, *Pseudomonas* | Souring, off-flavor |
| Bread (4.5) | 5.0–6.0 | 0.94 | *Bacillus*, *Rhizopus*, *Penicillium* | Ropy bread, mold |
| Apple (3.5) | 3.0–4.0 | 0.98 | *Penicillium expansum*, *Alternaria* | Blue mold, soft rot |
| Canned meat (5.5) | 6.0 | 0.97 | *Clostridium botulinum* | Bombage, toxin |
| Honey (4.0) | 3.9–4.5 | 0.55–0.75 | *Saccharomyces*, osmophilic yeasts | Fermentation |
| Dried fruits (15) | 3.5–4.5 | 0.60–0.75 | *Aspergillus*, xerophilic molds | Mold, mycotoxin |
| Butter (0.5) | 6.1–6.4 | 0.93 | *Pseudomonas*, *Molds* | Rancidity, mold |

---

## 3.4 Comparative Decision Logic Flow

```
START: Food Product Selected
         │
         ▼
   Check pH < 4.5 ?
    ┌────┴────┐
   YES       NO
    │         │
    ▼         ▼
  ACID   Check a_w < 0.85 ?
  FOOD     ┌─────┴─────┐
    │     YES         NO
    ▼     │           ▼
 Fungi   DRY       Check T < 5°C?
 dominate  │       ┌─────┴─────┐
    │     ▼      YES         NO
    ▼   Xerophilic│          ▼
  Molds/ molds    ▼      Mesophilic
  Yeasts dominate Psychrotrophs  spoilage
                 dominate    │
                      │      ▼
                      ▼    Bacteria
                  Molds dominate
                  dominate (Pseu, Salm)
```

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **KTU Key Insight:** The intrinsic and extrinsic factors form a **hierarchical filter**. Microbes must pass ALL filters (pH, $a_w$, T, Eh) to grow. Eliminating any one factor prevents spoilage.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Master Block Diagram: Spoilage Pathway Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fresh Food Substrate] --> B[Sterile Initial State]
    B --> C{Exposure to Microbes}
    C --> D[Environmental Conditions Check]
    D --> E1[pH Factor]
    D --> E2[Water Activity Factor]
    D --> E3[Temperature Factor]
    D --> E4[Oxygen Availability Factor]
    D --> E5[Nutrient Composition Factor]
    
    E1 --> F{Microbial Compatibility}
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    E5 --> F
    
    F -->|Bacteria Compatible| G1[Bacterial Spoilage Path]
    F -->|Mold Compatible| G2[Mold Spoilage Path]
    F -->|Yeast Compatible| G3[Yeast Spoilage Path]
    
    G1 --> H1[Proteolysis Putrefaction]
    G1 --> H2[Carbohydrate Fermentation]
    G1 --> H3[Lipolysis Rancidity]
    G1 --> H4[Toxin Production]
    
    G2 --> H5[Visible Mycelium Formation]
    G2 --> H6[Mycotoxin Production]
    G2 --> H7[Soft Rot Maceration]
    
    G3 --> H8[Alcoholic Fermentation]
    G3 --> H9[Gas Production]
    G3 --> H10[Off-flavor Production]
    
    H1 --> I[End Product Spoiled Food]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    H4 --> I
    H5 --> I
    H6 --> I
    H7 --> I
    H8 --> I
    H9 --> I
    H10 --> I
    
    I --> J{Safety Evaluation}
    J -->|Unsafe| K[Discard Pathogen Toxins]
    J -->|Conditionally Acceptable| L[Reprocess]
    J -->|Unacceptable| M[Destroy]
```

## 4.2 Classification Hierarchy of Food Spoilage Microorganisms

```mermaid
graph TD
    Root[Food Spoilage Microorganisms]
    
    Root --> BranchA[Bacteria Domain]
    Root --> BranchB[Fungi Domain]
    
    BranchA --> A1[Gram-Positive Bacteria]
    BranchA --> A2[Gram-Negative Bacteria]
    
    A1 --> A1a[Bacillus subtilis]
    A1 --> A1b[Bacillus cereus]
    A1 --> A1c[Clostridium botulinum]
    A1 --> A1d[Clostridium perfringens]
    A1 --> A1e[Staphylococcus aureus]
    A1 --> A1f[Listeria monocytogenes]
    A1 --> A1g[Lactobacillus spp]
    
    A2 --> A2a[Pseudomonas aeruginosa]
    A2 --> A2b[Pseudomonas fluorescens]
    A2 --> A2c[Salmonella enterica]
    A2 --> A2d[Erwinia carotovora]
    A2 --> A2e[Enterobacter aerogenes]
    A2 --> A2f[Alcaligenes spp]
    
    BranchB --> B1[Molds Filamentous]
    BranchB --> B2[Yeasts Unicellular]
    
    B1 --> B1a[Aspergillus flavus]
    B1 --> B1b[Aspergillus niger]
    B1 --> B1c[Penicillium roqueforti]
    B1 --> B1d[Penicillium expansum]
    B1 --> B1e[Rhizopus stolonifer]
    B1 --> B1f[Mucor mucedo]
    B1 --> B1g[Botrytis cinerea]
    B1 --> B1h[Fusarium graminearum]
    B1 --> B1i[Alternaria alternata]
    B1 --> B1j[Geotrichum candidum]
    
    B2 --> B2a[Saccharomyces cerevisiae]
    B2 --> B2b[Candida albicans]
    B2 --> B2c[Zygosaccharomyces rouxii]
    B2 --> B2d[Debaryomyces hansenii]
```

## 4.3 Mechanism Flow – Putrefaction of Protein

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Protein] -->|Protease Enzyme| P1[Polypeptides]
    P1 -->|Peptidase| P2[Amino Acids]
    P2 -->|Decarboxylase| P3[Biogenic Amines]
    P2 -->|Deaminase| P4[Ammonia NH3]
    P2 -->|Desulfhydrase| P5[Hydrogensulfide H2S]
    P3 --> P6[Putrescine Cadaverine Histamine]
    P4 --> P7[Foul Odor]
    P5 --> P7
    P6 --> P8[Putrid Off-Flavor]
    P7 --> P8
    P8 --> P9[Detected as Spoilage]
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture – Spoilage Prevention Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Spoilage Risk Assessment] --> T1[Intrinsic Barrier]
    S --> T2[Extrinsic Barrier]
    S --> T3[Implicit Barrier]
    
    T1 --> T11[Control pH]
    T1 --> T12[Reduce aw]
    T1 --> T13[Modify Eh]
    T1 --> T14[Add Antimicrobials]
    
    T2 --> T21[Refrigeration]
    T2 --> T22[Freezing]
    T2 --> T23[Modified Atmosphere]
    T2 --> T24[Heat Treatment]
    
    T3 --> T31[Hurdle Technology]
    T3 --> T32[Packaging]
    T3 --> T33[Sanitation]
    
    T11 --> R[Stable Shelf Life Achieved]
    T12 --> R
    T13 --> R
    T14 --> R
    T21 --> R
    T22 --> R
    T23 --> R
    T24 --> R
    T31 --> R
    T32 --> R
    T33 --> R
```

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **Figure Interpretation:** Modern food preservation employs **Hurdle Technology** (Leistner, 1978) — combining multiple mild preservation factors (low pH + reduced $a_w$ + refrigeration + modified atmosphere) to inhibit spoilage without compromising food quality.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — SHORT ANSWER QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between bacterial and fungal food spoilage with two examples each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Bacterial Spoilage | Fungal Spoilage |
|---|---|---|
| Organism Type | Unicellular, prokaryotic | Multicellular (molds) / unicellular (yeasts), eukaryotic |
| pH Range | Mostly neutral (5.5–7.5) | Acid-tolerant (3.5–6.0) |
| Moisture | Require higher $a_w$ ($\geq 0.91$) | Grow at lower $a_w$ ($\geq 0.80$) |
| Visible Signs | Slime, gas, putrid odor | Fuzzy growth, pigmentation |
| Examples | *Pseudomonas* in meat; *Bacillus* in bread (ropy) | *Aspergillus* on grains; *Penicillium* on fruits |

*(Mark distribution: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for table comparison, 1 mark for examples — 3 marks total)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is water activity ($a_w$)? List the minimum $a_w$ required for growth of bacteria, yeasts, and molds.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Water activity ($a_w$) is defined as the ratio of the partial vapour pressure of water in a food substrate to that of pure water at the same temperature. It measures the **availability of water** for microbial growth.
$$a_w = \dfrac{P_{solution}}{P^{\circ}_{water}}$$

**Minimum $a_w$ values (2 marks):**
| Organism | Minimum $a_w$ |
|---|---|
| Most spoilage bacteria | **0.91** |
| Most yeasts | **0.88** |
| Most molds | **0.80** |
| Halophilic bacteria | as low as **0.75** |
| Xerophilic molds | as low as **0.65** |

---

## PART B — LONG ANSWER QUESTIONS (14 MARKS EACH — INTERNAL CHOICE)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Discuss in detail the different types of bacterial spoilage of foods with suitable examples. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):** Bacterial spoilage refers to the deterioration of food caused by bacterial metabolic activity, resulting in off-odors, off-flavors, texture changes, slime formation, gas production, or visible colony growth.

**Classification of Bacterial Spoilage (6 marks):**

**1. Spoilage of Proteinaceous Foods (Putrefaction) (1.5 marks)**
- Caused by: *Clostridium* spp., *Pseudomonas* spp., *Bacillus* spp.
- Mechanism: Proteins → polypeptides → amino acids → biogenic amines (putrescine, cadaverine), NH₃, H₂S
- Example: Spoilage of canned meat by *Clostridium botulinum* producing H₂S, foul odor, and botulinum toxin; meat putrefaction by *Pseudomonas*.

**2. Spoilage of Carbohydrate Foods (Fermentation) (1.5 marks)**
- Caused by: *Lactobacillus*, *Streptococcus*, *Leuconostoc*
- Mechanism: Sugars → organic acids (lactic, acetic), CO₂, ethanol
- Example: Souring of milk by *Lactobacillus*; ropy bread by *Bacillus subtilis* producing dextran capsules.

**3. Spoilage of Fatty Foods (Lipolysis/Rancidity) (1.5 marks)**
- Caused by: *Pseudomonas*, *Achromobacter*, *Serratia*
- Mechanism: Lipases break triglycerides → free fatty acids → peroxides, aldehydes
- Example: Butter rancidity, oily fish spoilage producing oily/soapy off-flavors.

**4. Spoilage of Vegetables and Fruits (Soft Rot) (1.5 marks)**
- Caused by: *Erwinia carotovora* (pectinolytic), *Pseudomonas marginalis*
- Mechanism: Pectinases degrade plant cell wall pectins → tissue maceration, watery rot
- Example: Soft rot of potatoes, carrots in storage.

---

**(b) Explain the various intrinsic and extrinsic factors affecting food spoilage with their threshold values. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):** Food spoilage depends on a combination of intrinsic factors (within the food) and extrinsic factors (in the storage environment). These factors act as barriers to microbial growth.

**Intrinsic Factors (3.5 marks):**

**1. pH (1 mark):**
- Most bacteria grow optimally at pH 6.5–7.5
- Fungi (yeasts/molds) grow at pH 4.5–6.0
- Foods with pH < 4.5 (acid foods) are generally resistant to bacterial spoilage

**2. Water Activity ($a_w$) (1 mark):**
$$a_w = \dfrac{P_{food}}{P^{\circ}_{water}}$$
- Bacteria: minimum $a_w \approx 0.91$
- Yeasts: minimum $a_w \approx 0.88$
- Molds: minimum $a_w \approx 0.80$
- Reducing $a_w$ through drying/salting preserves food

**3. Redox Potential (Eh) (0.5 mark):**
- Aerobes require positive Eh (e.g., +200 mV)
- Anaerobes require negative Eh (e.g., $-200$ mV)
- Vacuum packaging lowers Eh → favours anaerobic spoilage

**4. Nutrient Content (0.5 mark):**
- High protein → bacterial spoilage; High sugar → mould/yeast spoilage
- Low-nutrient foods (sugar, oils) → limited growth

**5. Antimicrobial Constituents (0.5 mark):**
- Lysozyme, lactoferrin, lactoperoxidase in milk
- Essential oils in spices (eugenol, thymol)
- Benzoic acid, sorbic acid naturally in berries

**Extrinsic Factors (2.5 marks):**

**1. Temperature (1 mark):**
- Psychrotrophs (0–7 °C): *Pseudomonas*, *Listeria*
- Mesophiles (20–45 °C): *Salmonella*, *Staphylococcus*
- Thermophiles (>45 °C): rare spoilage organisms

**2. Relative Humidity (0.5 mark):**
- High RH (>90%) → mold growth on surfaces
- Low RH → drying of food surface

**3. Atmospheric Composition (1 mark):**
- O₂ favours aerobic spoilage (*Pseudomonas*, molds)
- Vacuum or CO₂-modified atmosphere inhibits aerobic spoilage
- MAP extends shelf life significantly

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Describe the role of molds in food spoilage. Explain any three important mycotoxins with their health effects. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):** Molds are multicellular filamentous fungi that grow on food surfaces, causing visible spoilage and producing toxic secondary metabolites called **mycotoxins**. They are major spoilage agents of stored grains, fruits, nuts, and bakery products.

**Major Spoilage Molds (2 marks):**
- *Aspergillus niger* — black mold on fruits, bread
- *Aspergillus flavus* — yellow-green mold on peanuts, maize
- *Penicillium expansum* — blue mold on apples, citrus
- *Penicillium roqueforti* — blue-green mold in cheese (intentional use)
- *Rhizopus stolonifer* — black bread mold
- *Fusarium graminearum* — pink mold on cereals
- *Botrytis cinerea* — gray mold on grapes

**Three Important Mycotoxins (4 marks):**

**1. Aflatoxin B₁ (1.5 marks):**
- Produced by: *Aspergillus flavus*, *A. parasiticus*
- Target foods: Peanuts, maize, corn, tree nuts, spices
- Optimal conditions: $a_w = 0.85$, T = 25–30 °C
- Health effects: **Hepatotoxic**; IARC Group 1 carcinogen → hepatocellular carcinoma; acute aflatoxicosis → liver failure
- Regulatory limit: <20 μg/kg in food

**2. Ochratoxin A (1.5 marks):**
- Produced by: *Aspergillus ochraceus*, *Penicillium verrucosum*
- Target foods: Cereals, coffee, grapes, dried fruits, wine
- Health effects: **Nephrotoxic** → Balkan endemic nephropathy; immunosuppression; possible carcinogen (IARC Group 2B)

**3. Patulin (1 mark):**
- Produced by: *Penicillium expansum*
- Target foods: Rotten apples, apple juice, damaged fruits
- Health effects: Gastrointestinal disturbances, neurotoxicity, possible carcinogen

**(Other acceptable mycotoxins:** Trichothecenes, Zearalenone, Fumonisins, Citrinin, Sterigmatocystin**.)**

---

**(b) Discuss the spoilage of specific foods: milk, meat, bread, and canned foods with their causative microorganisms. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Milk Spoilage (2 marks):**
- **Souring**: *Lactobacillus* spp., *Streptococcus lactis* → lactic acid → sour taste, coagulation
- **Sweet curdling**: *Bacillus cereus*, *Streptococcus* → renin-like enzymes curdle milk without acid
- **Ropiness**: *Alcaligenes viscosus*, *Enterobacter* → capsular polysaccharides → slimy strands
- **Proteolysis**: *Pseudomonas fluorescens*, *Bacillus* → bitter taste from peptide breakdown
- **Lipolysis**: *Pseudomonas*, *Achromobacter* → lipases → rancidity

**2. Meat Spoilage (2 marks):**
- **Surface slime**: *Pseudomonas*, *Acinetobacter*, *Moraxella* at refrigeration T
- **Putrefaction**: *Clostridium* spp. (anaerobic) → H₂S, indole, foul odor
- **Discoloration**: *Serratia marcescens* (red), *Pseudomonas* (green/blue pigments)
- **Bone taint**: Anaerobic *Clostridium* in deep tissue of carcasses
- **Greening**: *Leuconostoc* → H₂O₂ reacting with myoglobin → green color

**3. Bread Spoilage (1.5 marks):**
- **Ropy/moldy bread**: *Bacillus subtilis* → dextran capsule → sticky strings; *Rhizopus stolonifer* → black mold
- **Chalky bread**: *Endomyces fibuliger* → white chalky spots
- **Red bread**: *Serratia marcescens* → red pigment
- **Blue-green mold**: *Penicillium* spp.

**4. Canned Food Spoilage (1.5 marks):**
- **Flat sour**: *Bacillus stearothermophilus*, *B. coagulans* → acid without gas (thermophilic)
- **Swell/bombage (H₂ gas)**: *Clostridium thermosaccharolyticum* → gas + acid
- **Sulphur stinkers**: *Clostridium nigrificans* → H₂S + black FeS deposits
- **Botulism**: *Clostridium botulinum* → fatal neurotoxin (most dangerous)

---

> [!WARNING] 
> **KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING — COMMON PITFALLS:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing molds with yeasts**: Molds are **filamentous** (with hyphae/mycelium); yeasts are **unicellular** (e.g., *Saccharomyces*). Examiners deduct 1 mark for this confusion.
> 
> 2. **Incorrect $a_w$ values**: Memorize the values — **0.91 (bacteria), 0.88 (yeast), 0.80 (mold)**. Wrong values = 1 mark deduction.
> 
> 3. **Not naming specific species**: Writing just "Aspergillus" instead of "*Aspergillus flavus*" loses 0.5 mark. Be specific!
> 
> 4. **Omitting toxin information**: For any mold question, ALWAYS mention the **mycotoxin** it produces (Aflatoxin B₁ from *A. flavus*, Patulin from *P. expansum*, etc.).
> 
> 5. **Confusing spoilage with poisoning**: *Spoilage* = aesthetic/quality deterioration (off-flavor); *Poisoning* = health hazard (toxin/infection). Know the difference!
> 
> 6. **Forgetting intrinsic vs. extrinsic**: Always categorize factors — examiners look for this classification.
> 
> 7. **Missing examples**: Each factor or organism MUST have at least ONE named food example. Generic answers lose marks.

---

## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

### ⭐ Essential Concepts to Remember

- **Spoilage Definition**: Undesirable changes making food unfit for consumption
- **Spoilage Triangle**: Substrate + Microbe + Environment (all three required)
- **Three Bacterial Categories**:
  - *Gram-positive*: *Bacillus*, *Clostridium*, *Staphylococcus*, *Lactobacillus*, *Listeria*
  - *Gram-negative*: *Pseudomonas*, *Salmonella*, *Erwinia*, *Enterobacter*
- **Major Fungal Spoilage Agents**:
  - *Aspergillus* → Aflatoxin B₁ (liver cancer)
  - *Penicillium* → Patulin, Citrinin
  - *Fusarium* → Trichothecenes, Fumonisins
  - *Rhizopus* → Black bread mold

### ⭐ Critical Numerical Values (Must Memorize)

- Minimum $a_w$ for bacteria: **0.91**
- Minimum $a_w$ for yeasts: **0.88**
- Minimum $a_w$ for molds: **0.80**
- $D_{121}$ value for *C. botulinum*: **0.21 min**
- Optimum pH for bacterial growth: **6.5–7.5**
- Optimum pH for fungal growth: **4.5–6.0**
- Psychrotroph optimum: **3–7 °C**
- Mesophile optimum: **20–45 °C**

### ⭐ Key Formulas (Must Memorize)

- $a_w = \dfrac{P_{solution}}{P^{\circ}_{water}} = \dfrac{ERH}{100}$
- $\text{pH} = -\log[\text{H}^+]$
- $N = N_0 \cdot 2^n$
- $n = \dfrac{\log N - \log N_0}{0.301}$
- $g = \dfrac{t}{n}$
- $\mu = \dfrac{\ln 2}{g}$

### ⭐ Spoilage Mechanisms Summary

| Substrate | Process | End Products | Organisms |
|---|---|---|---|
| Proteins | Putrefaction | Amines, H₂S, NH₃ | *Clostridium*, *Pseudomonas* |
| Carbohydrates | Fermentation | Lactic acid, CO₂ | *Lactobacillus*, *Saccharomyces* |
| Lipids | Rancidity | FFAs, aldehydes | *Pseudomonas*, molds |
| Pectins | Soft rot | Tissue maceration | *Erwinia*, *Botrytis* |

### ⭐ High-Yield Exam Quick-Fire Facts

1. *Pseudomonas* = **psychrotroph** (cold-tolerant spoilage)
2. *Aspergillus flavus* = **Aflatoxin B₁** (most potent natural hepatocarcinogen)
3. *Clostridium botulinum* = **botulinum toxin** (most lethal food toxin)
4. *Erwinia carotovora* = **pectinase** (soft rot of vegetables)
5. *Penicillium expansum* = **Patulin** (apple juice contamination)
6. *Bacillus subtilis* = **dextran capsule** (ropy bread)
7. *Listeria monocytogenes* = grows at **0–45 °C** (cold tolerance)
8. *Rhizopus stolonifer* = **black bread mold**
9. *Fusarium* = **fumonisins** (esophageal cancer)
10. *Saccharomyces* = wild yeast causing **unwanted fermentation**

### ⭐ Examiner's Last-Minute Tips

- ✅ Always write **genus + species** (italics with underline in handwriting)
- ✅ Include **mechanism + example + consequence** for every organism
- ✅ Categorize spoilage as **physical, chemical, biological** when relevant
- ✅ Use the term **"biogenic amines"** (putrescine, cadaverine) for protein spoilage
- ✅ Mention **Hurdle Technology** when discussing preservation
- ✅ Connect **$a_w$, pH, T, Eh** to specific organism tolerances

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **FINAL KTU BOARD FOCUS:** Of the 14-mark questions, expect either: (1) "Discuss bacterial spoilage with examples" or (2) "Explain spoilage with reference to specific foods." Be prepared to write **3–4 pages** with diagrams, tables, and proper scientific terminology.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Food Preservation Techniques: Physical and Chemical Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT303 - ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Food Preservation Techniques: Physical and Chemical Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Food Preservation** is the process of treating and handling food to stop or greatly slow down spoilage, loss of nutritional value, quality, and edibility caused by the action of **microorganisms, endogenous enzymes, chemical reactions (oxidation), and physical/mechanical damage**, thereby extending the **shelf-life** of food products while maintaining their sensory, nutritional, and safety attributes.

The KTU 2024 scheme frames preservation under two broad operational umbrellas:
- **Physical Methods** (Thermal, Non-thermal, Mechanical)
- **Chemical Methods** (Natural and Synthetic Antimicrobials/Antioxidants)

## 1.2 Underlying Principles of Food Preservation

Every preservation technique, regardless of its physical or chemical nature, is governed by one or more of the following **principles of hurdle technology (Leistner, 1978)**:

1. **Inhibition of microbial growth** (lowering water activity, pH, redox potential).
2. **Inactivation of microbes and enzymes** (heat, irradiation, high pressure).
3. **Prevention of chemical deterioration** (oxidation, Maillard browning).
4. **Physical removal of contaminants** (washing, filtration, peeling).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The concept of **Hurdle Technology (Hurdle Effect)** is a favourite 7-14 mark question topic. Always remember that a combination of several mild preservation methods (hurdles) is more effective and nutrient-friendly than a single severe treatment.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bank Locker Analogy"

Imagine your freshly prepared food item is a **room full of precious gold bars** (nutrients, flavour, water). Three types of "thieves" want to break in:
- **Burglars** → Bacteria, yeast, mould (microbes).
- **Insects** → Enzymes (they eat the food from the inside).
- **Rust** → Oxidation and chemical reactions (slowly corrode quality).

A food preservation technique acts as a **bank security system**:
- Some methods build a **strong wall** (sterilization, irradiation) to keep thieves out.
- Some methods **freeze the thieves** (refrigeration, freezing).
- Some methods **dry out the room** (drying, salting, sugaring) so thieves cannot survive.
- Some methods **bribe the burglars** with chemical preservatives to keep them dormant.

Just as banks use multiple security layers, modern food science uses **multiple hurdles** for safe preservation.

## 1.4 Major Spoilage Agents — The "Enemies" of Food

| Spoilage Agent | Mechanism | Example Spoilage |
|---|---|---|
| **Bacteria** | Aerobic/Anaerobic respiration | Milk turning sour (*Lactobacillus*) |
| **Yeast & Moulds** | Fermentation, pH reduction | Bread mould (*Rhizopus*) |
| **Endogenous Enzymes** | Autolysis, proteolysis | Mushy overripe fruits (polygalacturonase) |
| **Oxidation (Lipid)** | Rancidity (Peroxides) | Stale butter, oxidized oils |
| **Maillard Reaction** | Non-enzymatic browning | Darkening of stored milk powder |
| **Insects & Rodents** | Physical contamination | Weevils in flour |

> [!TIP]
> **Standard metrics used in KTU exam answers:**
> - **Water Activity ($a_w$):** dimensionless, ranges from **0.00 (bone-dry)** to **1.00 (pure water)**.
> - **Shelf-life:** Measured in **days, weeks, or months**.
> - **Thermal Death Time (TDT):** Minutes at a given temperature.
> - **D, Z, F values:** Minutes and °C (with **D in min, Z in °C, F in min**).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Food Preservation Methods (Module 4 Scope)

### A. Physical Methods

#### A.1 Thermal Processing
Thermal methods use heat to inactivate microorganisms and enzymes. They are sub-divided by temperature regime:

**1. Pasteurization (LTLT, HTST, UHT)**
- **LTLT (Low Temperature, Long Time):** **63 °C for 30 min** (Batch, Holder method) — used for milk, fruit juices.
- **HTST (High Temperature, Short Time):** **72 °C for 15 s** (Plate heat exchanger) — industrial milk pasteurization.
- **UHT (Ultra-High Temperature):** **135–150 °C for 2–5 s** (Indirect/Direct) — gives shelf-stable milk.

**2. Sterilization (Commercial Sterility)**
- **In-container sterilization (Canning):** **121 °C for 15 min** at **15 psi** in retort.
- **Aseptic processing:** **140 °C for few seconds** + aseptic packaging.

**3. Blanching**
- Heating to **75–95 °C for 1–5 min**, then rapid cooling.
- Purpose: Inactivate **catalase, peroxidase, polyphenol oxidase** (enzymes).
- Used as **pre-treatment** before freezing, drying, or canning.

**4. Evaporation and Concentration**
- Removal of water via boiling (**100 °C**) or vacuum evaporation (**40–60 °C**).
- Used for jams, tomato ketchup, condensed milk.

#### A.2 Low Temperature Preservation
- **Refrigeration (Chilling):** **0–4 °C** (does NOT freeze water, slows microbes).
- **Freezing:** **−18 °C to −40 °C** (converts water to ice, halts all activity).
- **Cryopreservation:** **−196 °C** (liquid nitrogen) for long-term storage.

#### A.3 Dehydration / Drying
Removal of water to lower **$a_w$ < 0.6** (mould cannot grow).
Methods: **Sun drying, Tray drying, Spray drying, Drum drying, Fluidized bed, Freeze-drying (Lyophilization), Osmotic dehydration.**

#### A.4 Non-Thermal Physical Methods
- **Irradiation:** Ionizing ($\gamma$-rays, e-beam, X-rays) & Non-ionizing (UV, Microwave).
- **High Pressure Processing (HPP):** **400–600 MPa** for 1–15 min.
- **Pulsed Electric Field (PEF):** **20–80 kV/cm** short pulses.
- **Membrane Processing:** Microfiltration, Ultrafiltration, Reverse Osmosis.
- **Hurdle Technology** (a combination concept rather than a single technique).

### B. Chemical Methods

| Method | Active Principle | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Salting** | Reduces $a_w$ by osmotic effect | Pickled cucumber, salted fish |
| **Sugaring** | Reduces $a_w$, plasmolysis | Jams, jellies, preserves |
| **Smoking** | Phenols, formaldehyde, dehydration | Smoked meat, fish |
| **Pickling (Acidification)** | Lower pH < 4.5 inhibits pathogens | Vinegar pickles, sauerkraut |
| **Sulfiting** | SO₂ inhibits microbes & browning | Wines, dried fruits |
| **Benzoates** | Inhibit TCA cycle, cell wall | Sodium benzoate in juices |
| **Sorbates** | Inhibit moulds, yeasts | Potassium sorbate in cheese |
| **Nitrites / Nitrates** | Inhibit *Clostridium botulinum* | Cured meats |
| **Antioxidants (BHA, BHT)** | Scavenge free radicals | Fats, oils, snacks |
| **Natural antimicrobials** | Bacteriocins, essential oils | Nisin, citric acid, chitosan |

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are the most frequently tested numerical and theoretical problems in KTU ESE.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Water Activity | $a_w = \dfrac{p}{p_0}$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Decimal Reduction Time (D-value) | $\log\!\left(\dfrac{N_0}{N}\right) = \dfrac{t}{D}$ | $D$ in min, $t$ in min |
| 3 | D-value Definition | Time for 90% kill (1-log reduction) at constant $T$ | $D_T$ depends on T |
| 4 | Z-value | $Z = \dfrac{T_1 - T_2}{\log D_1 - \log D_2}$ | $Z$ in °C |
| 5 | Z-value Definition | Temperature change for 1-log change in D | Typical **Z = 10 °C** for many bacteria |
| 6 | Thermal Death Time (TDT) | $\log(\text{TDT}) = -\dfrac{T}{Z} + \text{constant}$ | min |
| 7 | F-value (Process lethality) | $F = 10^{\frac{T_{ref} - T}{Z}} \cdot t$ | min, $T_{ref}$ = 121.1 °C for *C. botulinum* |
| 8 | F₀ value (canning) | $F_0 = \sum 10^{\frac{T - 250}{z}} \cdot \Delta t$ | min, $z=10$, T in °F (250 °F ≈ 121.1 °C) |
| 9 | Arrhenius Equation | $k = A \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | $E_a$ in J/mol, R = **8.314 J/(mol·K)** |
| 10 | Q₁₀ Rule | $Q_{10} = \left(\dfrac{k_2}{k_1}\right)^{\frac{10}{T_2-T_1}}$ | For 10 °C rise |
| 11 | Spoilage Rate | $r = k \cdot C^n$ | Zero, 1st, or n-th order |
| 12 | Shelf-life | $t_s = \dfrac{\ln(C_0/C_{acc})}{k}$ | $C_{acc}$ = acceptable limit |
| 13 | Mass transfer during drying | $\dfrac{\partial M}{\partial t} = D_{eff} \cdot \nabla^2 M$ | Fick's 2nd law |
| 14 | Moisture content (dry basis) | $M_{db} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_d} \times 100$ | % |
| 15 | Humidity Ratio (air drying) | $Y = 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{p_w}{p - p_w}$ | kg water/kg dry air |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Canning Industry:** Uses F₀ ≥ 3 min to achieve **12-log reduction of *Clostridium botulinum***.
- **Dairy Plants:** HTST pasteurization ensures **5-log reduction of *Coxiella burnetii***.
- **Aerospace & Defence Meals:** Retort pouches sterilized to F₀ = 6–8 min for **ambient-stable shelf life of 5 years**.
- **Pharmaceutical Lyophilization:** Freeze-drying maintains 97% viability of live attenuated vaccines.
- **Export of Mango Pulp (India → EU):** Hot-break + aseptic packaging + F₀ = 4 min.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Decimal Reduction Time (D-Value)

### 3.1.1 Statement of the First-Order Inactivation Model

Microbial inactivation by heat (or any lethal agent) follows **first-order kinetics**, i.e., the rate of cell death is proportional to the viable cell count $N$ at any time $t$.

$$
\frac{dN}{dt} = -k \cdot N
$$

where:
- $N$ = number of viable cells at time $t$ (CFU/mL).
- $k$ = specific death rate constant (min⁻¹).
- $t$ = time of exposure (min).

### 3.1.2 Integration Step

Separating variables and integrating from initial count $N_0$ at $t=0$ to count $N$ at time $t$:

$$
\int_{N_0}^{N} \frac{dN}{N} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt
$$

$$
\ln(N) - \ln(N_0) = -k \cdot t
$$

### 3.1.3 Solving for N

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{N}{N_0}\right) = -k \cdot t
$$

$$
N = N_0 \cdot e^{-k t}
$$

Converting to base-10 logarithm (more convenient for D-value):

$$
\log(N) = \log(N_0) - \frac{k}{2.303} \cdot t
$$

### 3.1.4 Defining the D-Value

The **D-value** is defined as the time required for a **one-log (90%) reduction** in the microbial population, i.e., when $N/N_0 = 0.1$:

$$
\frac{N}{N_0} = 0.1
$$

$$
\log\!\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right) = 1
$$

Substituting into the integrated form:

$$
1 = \frac{t}{D} \quad \Rightarrow \quad D = \frac{1}{k / 2.303} = \frac{2.303}{k}
$$

Hence the **master D-value equation**:

$$
\log\!\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right) = \frac{t}{D}
$$

### 3.1.5 Worked Numerical Example

> A milk sample has initial bacterial count $N_0 = 10^6$ CFU/mL. After HTST pasteurization (72 °C, 15 s), the count is $N = 10^2$ CFU/mL. Calculate the D-value and the death rate constant $k$.

**Solution:**
Total log reduction:
$$
\log(N_0/N) = \log(10^6 / 10^2) = \log(10^4) = 4
$$

$$
D = \frac{t}{\log(N_0/N)} = \frac{0.25 \text{ min}}{4} = 0.0625 \text{ min} = 3.75 \text{ s}
$$

Death rate constant:
$$
k = \frac{2.303}{D} = \frac{2.303}{0.0625} = 36.85 \text{ min}^{-1}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Z-Value

The D-value changes with temperature. A 10 °C rise typically reduces D by 90% (i.e., one log). The Z-value is the temperature rise required for a 1-log change in D.

Starting from two D-values $D_1$ at $T_1$ and $D_2$ at $T_2$:

$$
\log(D_1) - \log(D_2) = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{Z}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
Z = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{\log D_1 - \log D_2}
$$

### 3.2.1 Worked Numerical Example

> At **121 °C**, $D_1 = 0.21$ min. At **111 °C**, $D_2 = 2.1$ min. Calculate Z.

$$
Z = \frac{121 - 111}{\log(0.21) - \log(2.1)} = \frac{10}{\log(0.1)} = \frac{10}{-1} = -10 \text{ °C}
$$

The negative sign indicates a negative slope (D decreases as T increases). By convention, Z is reported as **10 °C**.

## 3.3 Derivation of F-Value (Thermal Process Lethality)

The F-value is the **equivalent time at a reference temperature ($T_{ref}$)** that produces the same lethality as the actual time-temperature profile. 

Starting from the definition:
$$
F = t \cdot 10^{\frac{T - T_{ref}}{Z}}
$$

For canned foods, $T_{ref} = 121.1$ °C (250 °F), and Z = 10 °C. Substituting $Z = 10$ and $T_{ref} = 121.1$:

$$
F_0 = t \cdot 10^{\frac{T - 121.1}{10}}
$$

In imperial units (used by KTU textbook problems):

$$
F_0 = \sum_{i} 10^{\frac{T_i - 250}{z}} \cdot \Delta t_i
$$

where $T_i$ is in °F, $z$ is in °F (typically 18 °F, equivalent to 10 °C), and $\Delta t$ is in minutes.

### 3.3.1 Worked Numerical Example

> A retort reaches **125 °C (= 257 °F)** and holds for **8 minutes**. If $z = 18$ °F, calculate $F_0$.

$$
F_0 = 8 \times 10^{\frac{257 - 250}{18}} = 8 \times 10^{0.389} = 8 \times 2.45 = 19.6 \text{ min}
$$

This means the actual process lethality is equivalent to **19.6 minutes at 250 °F**, well above the FDA minimum of $F_0 = 3$ min for low-acid canned foods.

## 3.4 Derivation of Arrhenius Equation Application to Spoilage

The Arrhenius equation describes how a reaction rate constant varies with temperature:

$$
k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / (RT)}
$$

For shelf-life estimation at two temperatures:

$$
\frac{k_2}{k_1} = \exp\!\left[\frac{E_a}{R} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)\right]
$$

### 3.4.1 Worked Numerical Example

> The rate constant for ascorbic acid degradation in orange juice is $k_{25} = 0.05$ day⁻¹ at 25 °C. Calculate the shelf-life at 5 °C. Assume $E_a = 80$ kJ/mol and R = 8.314 J/(mol·K). Acceptable vitamin loss: 30% (i.e., $C/C_0 = 0.7$).

**Step 1:** Convert temperatures to Kelvin.
$T_1 = 25 + 273 = 298$ K
$T_2 = 5 + 273 = 278$ K

**Step 2:** Calculate ratio of rate constants.

$$
\frac{k_2}{k_1} = \exp\!\left[\frac{80000}{8.314} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{298} - \frac{1}{278}\right)\right]
$$

$$
\frac{1}{298} - \frac{1}{278} = \frac{278 - 298}{298 \times 278} = \frac{-20}{82844} = -2.414 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}
$$

$$
\frac{k_2}{k_1} = \exp\!\left[9622.3 \times (-2.414 \times 10^{-4})\right] = \exp(-2.323) = 0.098
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate $k_2$ at 5 °C.

$$
k_2 = 0.05 \times 0.098 = 0.0049 \text{ day}^{-1}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply first-order decay for 30% loss (i.e., $N/N_0 = 0.7$).

$$
t_s = -\frac{\ln(0.7)}{k_2} = \frac{0.357}{0.0049} = 72.8 \text{ days}
$$

So refrigeration at 5 °C extends the shelf-life of orange juice to **~73 days** from 25 °C's ~9 days.

## 3.5 Derivation of Water Activity and Microbial Inhibition Limits

Water activity determines the availability of water for microbial metabolism. It is defined as:

$$
a_w = \frac{p}{p_0} = \frac{\text{partial vapour pressure of water in food}}{\text{partial vapour pressure of pure water at same }T}
$$

Microbial growth limits (highly important for KTU 14-mark questions):

| Microorganism | Minimum $a_w$ for Growth |
|---|---|
| Most bacteria | 0.91 |
| Most yeasts | 0.88 |
| Most moulds | 0.80 |
| Halophilic bacteria | 0.75 |
| Xerophilic moulds | 0.65 |
| Osmophilic yeasts | 0.60 |
| **No microbial growth** | < 0.60 |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The relationship $a_w$ = 0.6 is the critical threshold below which **no microbial proliferation** is possible. This is why **dried fruits, honey, and intermediate-moisture foods** like jams are shelf-stable.

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Classification of Food Preservation Techniques

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    B --> B1[Thermal Processing]

    B --> B3[Dehydration and Drying]
    B --> B4[Non Thermal Physical]

    B1 --> B1a[Pasteurization LTLT HTST UHT]
    B1 --> B1b[Sterilization and Canning]
    B1 --> B1c[Blanching]
    B1 --> B1d[Evaporation and Concentration]

    B2 --> B2a[Refrigeration 0 to 4 C]
    B2 --> B2b[Freezing minus 18 C]

    B4 --> B4a[Irradiation Gamma UV]

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style - 3 Mark Module 4]`

### Question 1: Define water activity. How is it related to microbial growth in foods? 
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Water activity ($a_w$) is the ratio of the partial vapour pressure of water in a food substrate to the vapour pressure of pure water at the same temperature. It is dimensionless and ranges from 0 to 1.
$$a_w = \frac{p}{p_0}$$
- **Relation to microbial growth (1 mark):** Microorganisms require a minimum $a_w$ to grow. Bacteria need $a_w > 0.91$, yeasts $> 0.88$, moulds $> 0.80$. Below **$a_w = 0.60$**, no microbial growth occurs, which is the basis of preservation by drying, salting, and sugaring.

### Question 2: Differentiate between pasteurization and sterilization.
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Pasteurization | Sterilization |
|---|---|---|
| **Objective** | Kill pathogens only (not all spores) | Kill all microbes including spores |
| **Temperature** | 63–72 °C (LTLT/HTST) or 135 °C (UHT) | 121 °C and above |
| **Shelf-life** | Days to weeks (refrigerated) | Months to years (ambient) |
| **Microbial log reduction** | 5-log of pathogens | 12-log of *C. botulinum* |
| **Example** | Milk, fruit juice | Canned vegetables, meat |
| **Nutrient loss** | Minimal | Moderate to high |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Writing "sterilization kills 100% microbes" is wrong. Commercial sterilization in food achieves **commercial sterility** (no pathogens, low non-pathogenic spore count). The correct term is **12-D reduction of *C. botulinum***.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A: Thermal Processing Fundamentals** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]`

**(a)** Explain the principles of thermal processing of foods. Discuss in detail the methods of pasteurization and sterilization with their temperature-time combinations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Principles of Thermal Processing (3 marks):**
1. **Microbial inactivation** follows first-order kinetics.
2. Heat destroys **vegetative cells first**, then **spores** (with greater resistance).
3. **pH of food** determines the process intensity: low-acid foods (pH > 4.5) need sterilization; acidic foods (pH < 4.5) need only pasteurization.
4. Heat transfer to the slowest heating point in the food (cold spot) governs the process lethality.
5. Thermal Death Time (TDT) curves, D, Z, and F values are used to compute process requirements.

**Methods of Pasteurization (2 marks):**
- **Vat Pasteurization (LTLT):** 63 °C / 30 min → milk, cream.
- **HTST:** 72 °C / 15 s → continuous plate exchanger, used industrially.
- **UHT:** 135–150 °C / 2–5 s → aseptic packaging, 6-month ambient stability.

**Methods of Sterilization (2 marks):**
- **In-container (Retort):** 121.1 °C / 15 min at 15 psi (F₀ ≥ 3 min).
- **Aseptic processing:** 140 °C / 4 s + sterile filling into pre-sterilized containers (Tetra Pak).
- **Hydrostatic cooker:** Continuous sterilization at 121 °C for canned vegetables.

**(b)** Define D-value and Z-value. A bacterial population of $10^5$ CFU/mL is subjected to pasteurization. After 5 minutes, the count drops to $10^2$ CFU/mL. Calculate D-value and death rate constant. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definitions (2 marks):**
- **D-value (Decimal Reduction Time):** Time required at a given temperature to reduce the microbial population by 90% (1-log cycle), at a constant temperature.
- **Z-value:** The temperature change (in °C) required to change the D-value by a factor of 10 (1-log).

**Numerical Solution (5 marks):**

**Step 1:** Compute log reduction.
$$
\log\!\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right) = \log\!\left(\frac{10^5}{10^2}\right) = 3
$$

**Step 2:** Apply D-value formula.
$$
D = \frac{t}{\log(N_0/N)} = \frac{5 \text{ min}}{3} = 1.667 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute k.
$$
k = \frac{2.303}{D} = \frac{2.303}{1.667} = 1.382 \text{ min}^{-1}
$$

**Step 4:** Verify using exponential form.
$$
N = N_0 \cdot e^{-kt} = 10^5 \cdot e^{-1.382 \times 5} = 10^5 \cdot e^{-6.91} = 10^5 \times 0.001 = 100 = 10^2 \text{ CFU/mL} \;\; \checkmark
$$

**Final Answer:** **D = 1.667 min**, **k = 1.382 min⁻¹**.

---

### **Question B: Chemical Preservation and Low-Temperature Methods** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]`

**(a)** Describe the various chemical methods of food preservation with suitable examples. Explain the role of common chemical preservatives. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Salting (2 marks):**
- Salt ($NaCl$) reduces $a_w$ by osmotic dehydration.
- 15–25% salt inhibits most bacteria (especially putrefactive).
- Example: Salted fish, salted meat, pickles.
- Mechanism: Plasmolysis of microbial cells.

**2. Sugaring (1 mark):**
- Used in jams, jellies, marmalades.
- 60–65% sugar concentration reduces $a_w < 0.86$.
- Inhibits moulds and osmo-sensitive bacteria.

**3. Smoking (1 mark):**
- Combines dehydration with antimicrobial smoke phenols.
- Example: Smoked salmon, ham.

**4. Pickling/Acidification (1 mark):**
- Vinegar (acetic acid) lowers pH below 4.5.
- Lactic acid fermentation in sauerkraut, kimchi, yoghurt.

**5. Chemical Preservatives (2 marks):**
- **Sodium benzoate (0.1%):** Effective at pH < 4.5; inhibits moulds, yeasts, bacteria in juices.
- **Potassium sorbate:** Effective against moulds in cheese, cakes.
- **Sulfites (SO₂, 100–300 ppm):** Inhibit *Brettanomyces* in wines; prevent browning in dried fruits.
- **Nitrites (120 ppm in cured meat):** Inhibit *Clostridium botulinum*, fix colour.
- **Antioxidants (BHA, BHT, 0.02%):** Scavenge free radicals in oils, baked goods.

**(b)** Explain the principles of low-temperature preservation. Differentiate between refrigeration and freezing. Discuss the effect of freezing on food quality. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Principles (2 marks):**
- Microbial growth and enzymatic reactions slow with temperature drop.
- $Q_{10} \approx 2$ to 3 → for every 10 °C drop, spoilage rate halves.
- Freezing immobilizes water as ice, halting all biological activity.

**Refrigeration vs Freezing (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Refrigeration | Freezing |
|---|---|---|
| **Temperature** | 0 to 4 °C | −18 to −40 °C |
| **Water state** | Liquid | Solid (ice crystals) |
| **Microbial action** | Slowed, not stopped | Effectively stopped |
| **Enzymatic action** | Slowed | Inactivated slowly |
| **Shelf-life gain** | Days to weeks | Months to years |
| **Method** | Cold storage room | Blast freezer, plate freezer, cryogenic |
| **Examples** | Milk, fresh fruits | Frozen peas, meat, seafood |

**Effect of Freezing on Food Quality (2 marks):**
- **Ice crystal size matters:** Slow freezing → large extracellular crystals → cell rupture; Quick freezing → small intracellular crystals → better texture.
- **Freezer burn:** Sublimation of water from surface → dehydration, oxidation.
- **Thawing drip:** Loss of cell fluid upon thawing reduces nutritional value.
- **Protein denaturation** in fish and meat if frozen slowly.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not confuse D-value and Z-value units.** D is in **minutes**, Z is in **°C**. Many students write Z in minutes.
> 2. **The reference temperature for F₀ is 250 °F (121.1 °C), NOT 121 °C.** Off-by-one errors cost 1 mark.
> 3. **Logarithm base is 10 for D/Z/F but base e (or ln) for Arrhenius k.** Mixing bases is a frequent error.
> 4. **For 14-mark answers, always include labeled diagrams.** A canned food question without a retort block diagram typically loses 2 marks.
> 5. **Do not write "sterilization kills all microbes."** The correct KTU phrase is "achieves **commercial sterility** with **12-D reduction of *C. botulinum***."
> 6. **Hurdle technology** is worth 2 bonus marks if mentioned in any preservation answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision for KTU ESE — Module 4**

- ✅ **Definition:** Food preservation = extending shelf-life by inhibiting microbial, enzymatic, and chemical spoilage.
- ✅ **Two main classes:** Physical (heat, cold, dehydration, irradiation, HPP, PEF, membrane) and Chemical (salt, sugar, smoke, acid, benzoates, sorbates, sulfites, nitrites, antioxidants, bacteriocins).
- ✅ **Water activity ($a_w$):** $a_w = p/p_0$. Microbial growth halted below **$a_w = 0.60$**.
- ✅ **Pasteurization temperatures:** LTLT **63 °C/30 min**; HTST **72 °C/15 s**; UHT **135 °C/2–5 s**.
- ✅ **Sterilization:** **121.1 °C/15 min** at 15 psi, $F_0 \geq 3$ min for low-acid canned foods.
- ✅ **D-value:** Time for **90% (1-log) kill**; units **minutes**.
- ✅ **Z-value:** Temperature change for **1-log change in D**; units **°C** (typically 10 °C for bacteria).
- ✅ **F-value:** Equivalent time at $T_{ref}$ (121.1 °C) for same lethality; $F_0 = t \cdot 10^{(T-121.1)/Z}$.
- ✅ **Arrhenius:** $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a/(RT)}$, R = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**.
- ✅ **$Q_{10}$** rule: spoilage rate **doubles or triples per 10 °C rise**.
- ✅ **Hurdle technology** (Leistner 1978) uses multiple gentle hurdles; KTU favourite 7–14 mark question.
- ✅ **Benzoates** are effective only at **pH < 4.5**; sorbates work on moulds/yeasts; nitrites are specific to **cured meats**; sulfites prevent **browning and Brettanomyces**.
- ✅ **Blanching** inactivates **catalase, peroxidase, polyphenol oxidase** but NOT spores.
- ✅ **Spray drying** is best for heat-sensitive liquids; **freeze drying** for highest quality retention.
- ✅ **Freezing** is best at **−18 °C or below**; **refrigeration** is **0 to 4 °C**.
- ✅ **Irradiation doses:** Low (< 1 kGy) inhibits sprouting; Medium (1–10 kGy) kills pathogens; High (> 10 kGy) industrial sterilization.
- ✅ **HPP:** 400–600 MPa, ambient temperature, retains colour/flavour/vitamins.
- ✅ **Critical for 14-mark answers:** Include at least **1 block diagram + 1 numerical derivation + 1 comparison table**.
- ✅ **Avoid saying "100% sterile"** — use **"commercially sterile"** or **"12-D reduction of *C. botulinum*"**.

---

> **END OF MODULE 4 NOTES — FOOD PRESERVATION TECHNIQUES**
